reviews.review: This manuscript presents a thorough reanalysis of the ObjectNet benchmark, isolating objects via bounding boxes and segmentation masks to measure the true generalization and robustness of ImageNet-trained models. Strengths: (1) The paper provides a clear and reproducible methodology for annotating bounding boxes over the 113 overlapping categories and re‐evaluates six popular architectures, showing a consistent 10–15% top‐1 accuracy gain when models see only the object region. (2) The authors extend the analysis to synthetic corruptions, adversarial attacks (FGSM, PGD‐5), and geometric transformations, demonstrating that isolating the object improves robustness across the board. (3) The segmentation‐mask experiments on COCO classes convincingly argue that foreground subtraction yields further robustness benefits with minimal standard‐accuracy drop. (4) The qualitative study highlights real annotation challenges and human–machine difficulty alignment. Weaknesses: (1) Novelty is incremental, mostly refining an existing dataset rather than offering new modeling techniques. (2) The study lacks human performance baselines on the isolated‐object recognition task, making it hard to assess human–machine gaps. (3) Training from scratch on isolated objects is not explored, leaving open questions about dataset bias. (4) Multi‐label scenarios and full detection pipelines remain untested. Recommendations: (a) Include controlled human accuracy measurements on the cropped classes to ground model performance. (b) Explore end‐to‐end training or fine‐tuning on isolated‐object data to assess whether improved generalization persists. (c) Annotate multi‐label object presence to evaluate real‐world detection and recognition jointly. Overall, this work yields valuable insights into context’s role and suggests promising future research in segmentation‐based defenses.

reviews.review:
This manuscript presents a reanalysis of the recent ObjectNet benchmark by isolating objects via manually drawn bounding boxes and segmentation masks, and evaluates a range of state-of-the-art CNNs on classification accuracy and robustness to natural corruptions, geometric transformations, and adversarial attacks. The authors annotate 18,574 ObjectNet images in 113 overlapping ImageNet classes, demonstrate 10–15% top-1 accuracy gains (20–30% using their code) by cropping to object bounding boxes, and show further robustness improvements using segmentation masks on synthetic COCO images. A qualitative human vs. machine difficulty analysis is also provided.

Strengths:
• Comprehensive dataset annotation: bounding boxes on 18K images fill a gap in ObjectNet’s evaluation protocol.  
• Rigorous empirical study: tests on six architectures, 14 corruptions, FGSM/PGD attacks, and geometric shifts convincingly show benefits of foreground isolation.  
• Actionable insights: foreground subtraction and segmentation are proposed as practical defenses and research directions.  
• Qualitative analysis: ties human difficulty to model performance, highlighting challenging instances.

Weaknesses:
• Novelty is incremental: mainly a reanalysis rather than a new method.  
• Segmentation experiments rely on COCO masks, not ObjectNet masks, limiting direct applicability.  
• No human psychophysics baseline on isolated crops; human accuracy claims are anecdotal.  
• Lack of statistical significance testing and multilabel annotations for ObjectNet scenes.  
• Object-detector evaluation is only suggested, not performed.

Suggestions:
1. Include a small-scale human study on cropped vs. full ObjectNet images with isolated object labels.  
2. Provide statistical confidence intervals or repeated runs for robustness metrics.  
3. Explore lightweight segmentation model integration at inference on ObjectNet images.  
4. Annotate multi-object labels or compare to object-detection outputs to assess scene context effects.  
5. Release segmentation masks on ObjectNet or fine-tune a COCO-trained segmenter and report end-to-end results.

Overall, the paper offers valuable empirical insights but would benefit from deeper novelty, statistical rigor, and integration of segmentation on ObjectNet.

reviews.review:
Summary:
The paper revisits the ObjectNet benchmark, arguing that applying pretrained CNNs to manually cropped object bounding boxes (and further to segmentation masks) partially recovers the dramatic accuracy drop reported by Barbu et al. It systematically evaluates six ImageNet models on 113 overlapping categories under natural corruptions, adversarial attacks, and geometric shifts, showing 10–30% gains when restricting to object regions. A qualitative analysis suggests human/model difficulty aligns, and the authors advocate foreground extraction as a robustness strategy.

Strengths:
1. Thorough empirical reanalysis across multiple model architectures and perturbation types. 2. Insightful comparison of full‐image vs. bounding‐box vs. segmentation‐mask inputs, with consistent robustness improvements. 3. Release of annotation code and data for reproducibility. 4. Qualitative human‐machine difficulty alignment is interesting and underscores ObjectNet’s challenge.

Weaknesses:
1. Bounding‐box annotation is manual, limiting scalability; no evaluation of automated detectors to support deployment. 2. Statistical rigor is lacking: no confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or per‐category significance analysis. 3. Human performance is only quoted from Barbu et al.; no dedicated psychophysical study matching model conditions (cropped rapid classification). 4. Training on segmentation masks is limited to small COCO subset (10 classes, 700 images); generalization to full ObjectNet remains untested. 5. Discussion of confounds (e.g., different preprocessing, absence of test‐time augmentation) is cursory; inconsistent protocols impede fair comparison. 6. Minor formatting issues: broken footnotes, misplaced references in text, inconsistent figure labels, and variable image scales.

Actionable Suggestions:
- Include statistical tests (e.g., paired t-tests or bootstrapped CIs) to confirm reported gains.  
- Evaluate automated object detection or segmentation pipelines to demonstrate practical applicability.  
- Conduct a controlled human study on cropped ObjectNet samples to establish a proper baseline.  
- Expand segmentation mask training to more classes or use synthetic masks to assess scalability.  
- Standardize preprocessing and augmentations across comparisons, and report exact settings.  
- Clean up formatting: fix footnotes, unify figure/table captions, and ensure consistent reference formatting.

reviews.review:
Summary
This paper revisits Barbu et al.’s ObjectNet benchmark and demonstrates that isolating objects (via manual bounding boxes and segmentation masks) recovers 10–30% of the reported performance loss on state-of-the-art CNNs, yet a substantial gap to ImageNet remains. The authors further show that cropping to the object region improves robustness to natural distortions (noise, blur), geometric transformations, and white-box adversarial attacks. A qualitative analysis highlights human and model error patterns.

Strengths
1. Comprehensive empirical study across six architectures, 113 overlap classes, 14 distortion types, FGSM/PGD attacks, and geometric transforms. 2. Manual bounding box and mask annotations (18K images) and reproducible code. 3. Clear evidence that limiting context boosts both accuracy and multiple robustness metrics. 4. Actionable insight: foreground extraction should be integrated into recognition pipelines.

Weaknesses
1. Manual annotations restrict evaluation to 113 classes; results may not generalize to full ObjectNet. 2. No statistical significance tests or error bars on performance gains. 3. Human baseline for isolated-object recognition is missing. 4. The segmentation-based defense assumes perfect masks, which may not be available at scale.

Suggestions
1. Report confidence intervals or paired significance tests to validate improvements. 2. Conduct a small human psychophysics study on cropped vs. full scenes to benchmark human performance. 3. Explore automated mask extraction (e.g., off-the-shelf segmenters) to assess real-world applicability. 4. Extend analysis to the full 313 classes or compare to object detectors fine-tuned on similar data.

reviews.review: This paper introduces Graph Edit Networks (GENs), a novel output layer for graph neural networks that directly predicts sparse sequences of graph edits (node/edge insertions, deletions, relabels) to transform input graphs into target graphs. Strengths: (1) The edit‐based formulation elegantly handles general graph‐to‐graph prediction tasks, including node set changes, overcoming limitations of dense decoders or pure link‐prediction models. (2) Theoretical results rigorously connect graph mappings, edit scripts, and the graph edit distance, proving that, under optimal mappings and sufficiently expressive GNNs, GENs achieve near‐optimal edit sequences. (3) GENs operate in linear time for sparse graphs and make fully local decisions, avoiding costly pooling layers and quadratic decoders. (4) Proof‐of‐concept experiments demonstrate perfect or near‐perfect accuracy on three synthetic graph dynamical systems and two tree rewriting tasks, outperforming variational graph autoencoders and kernel‐based methods. Weaknesses: (1) Empirical evaluation is limited to small or synthetic datasets; real‐world dynamic graphs are not explored. (2) Constructing training signals requires computing or approximating graph mappings (GED), which can be expensive or domain‐specific. (3) The Markovian assumption restricts temporal context; integration with recurrent GNNs is only discussed. (4) Sensitivity to mapping quality or noisy examples is not assessed. Actionable suggestions: (1) Evaluate GENs on larger real‐world datasets (e.g., social or traffic networks) to validate scalability and generalization. (2) Include ablation studies on mapping quality by comparing different GED approximators and measuring the impact on edit accuracy. (3) Demonstrate robustness to imperfect mappings or partial supervision. (4) Explore extensions to non‐Markovian settings by combining GENs with sequence models. Overall, the paper offers a compelling new direction for graph‐to‐graph learning with solid theoretical foundations but would benefit from broader empirical validation.

reviews.review: This paper introduces the Graph Edit Network (GEN), a novel GNN output layer that predicts a sparse sequence of node and edge edits transforming one graph into another. The authors establish a rigorous training scheme via multi-step teaching signals derived from a node mapping, and prove that GEN’s edit scripts approach the graph edit distance up to an additive 2(C–1) term (C = # connected components). Empirical validation on synthetic graph dynamical systems (edit cycles, degree‐based rules, Game of Life), two tree rewriting tasks, and a max‐degree task shows that GEN achieves near‐perfect precision/recall on node and edge edits, substantially outperforming VGAE, VGRNN, and kernel‐based tree prediction. A scalability study on real citation data confirms linear inference time with edge filtering. Strengths: 1) Elegant linear output layer that handles node insertions/deletions and edge changes; 2) Strong theoretical guarantees linking GEN to graph edit distance; 3) Efficient, interpretable, local edit decisions; 4) Comprehensive synthetic benchmarks demonstrating high fidelity and scalability. Weaknesses: 1) Reliance on an external graph mapping oracle for training; real‐world mappings are NP‐hard to compute and may degrade performance; 2) Evaluation limited to synthetic and small‐scale data; no demonstration on real dynamic graph benchmarks (e.g., social networks, traffic); 3) Markovian assumption; extending to longer temporal contexts is not evaluated; 4) Training hyperparameters (margins, edge filters) require careful tuning; sensitivity analysis is missing. Actionable suggestions: • Include experiments on real dynamic graph datasets and compare to recent dynamic GNNs (Dyngraph2vec, EvolveGCN). • Provide an ablation on mapping quality (optimal vs. approximate) and threshold/β sensitivity. • Discuss or demonstrate integration with recurrent GNNs to relax the Markov assumption. • Detail the computational cost of mapping generation. Overall, GEN is innovative and well‐grounded; addressing these limitations would strengthen its real‐world applicability.

reviews.review:
Summary:
This paper introduces Graph Edit Networks (GENs), a novel output layer for GNNs that predicts sparse, local graph edits—insertions, deletions, relabels—to transform an input graph into its successor. The authors prove that given a node mapping, GEN can be trained to recover nearly optimal edit scripts (within 2·(C–1) of the graph edit distance, where C is the number of connected components). They propose a multi-step teaching signal construction, train end-to-end with a hinge or cross-entropy edit loss, and validate on synthetic dynamical systems, tree rewriting tasks, and demonstrate linear inference scaling with edge filtering.

Strengths:
• Novel formulation: learning explicit edit sequences addresses graph-to-graph prediction, covering topology and node-set changes.  
• Theoretical guarantees: mapping→teaching signal and near-optimality proofs are well crafted and extend prior GED literature.  
• Sparse, local output avoids quadratic decoding and pooling layers; experiments confirm perfect edit recall/precision on toy DSS and near-linear runtime on large citation snapshots.

Weaknesses:
• Reliance on precomputed node mappings or approximations (Hungarian/GED) limits applicability when ground truth correspondences are unavailable.  
• Evaluation restricted to synthetic benchmarks; no real-world dynamic graph task (e.g. evolving social, molecular graphs) to demonstrate generalization.  
• Baselines (VGAE, VGRNN) are not designed for node-set changes; comparison to dense graph-to-graph decoders or conditional generators would be more informative.  
• No ablation of multi-step teaching vs. single-step, nor assessment of error accumulation over multiple predictions.  
• Hyperparameter sensitivity (margin thresholds, edge filtering R) and training stability are not thoroughly explored.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Add experiments on a real dynamic graph dataset (e.g. traffic, social interactions) where node churn occurs, reporting graph similarity metrics over longer horizons.  
2. Evaluate GEN without access to oracle node mappings by integrating a learned matching network or by testing on datasets with identity labels withheld.  
3. Compare to a dense graph-to-graph VAE/Transformer decoder (e.g. GCPN, GraphRNN) on edit recall/precision and end-task accuracy.  
4. Include an ablation: single- vs. multi-step teaching signals, and measure how errors propagate when chaining predictions.  
5. Provide sensitivity analysis on margin parameter, edge filtering budget R, and the cost-weighted edit loss term, to guide practitioners.

Overall, GENs represent an elegant, theoretically grounded approach to sparse graph editing, but require stronger empirical validation on realistic tasks and deeper treatment of mapping-free training to fully justify acceptance.

reviews.review:
Summary. The paper introduces Graph Edit Networks (GEN), a novel output layer for graph neural networks that predicts sparse, local graph edits—node insertions, deletions, relabeling, and edge modifications—to transform one graph into another. The authors provide theoretical results that relate GEN output to near-optimal graph edit distance under an optimal node mapping, and propose training and inference schemes. They report proof-of-concept experiments on synthetic graph dynamical systems, tree transformations, and a citation network, showing high precision and recall for edit recovery compared to variational autoencoder baselines.

Strengths. Predicting explicit, sparse edit scripts is an original contribution that addresses graph-to-graph learning tasks including node set changes. The theoretical connection to edit distances and the construction of multi-step teaching signals are clearly presented. Empirical results on small synthetic benchmarks validate the feasibility of the approach and demonstrate orders-of-magnitude speedups under edge filtering.

Weaknesses. The evaluation is limited to small, synthetic or semi-synthetic tasks and omits stronger recent temporal GNN baselines. It relies on optimal or approximate node mappings that may not scale or generalize in large real-world graphs. The Markovian assumption and multi-step teaching protocol raise concerns about applicability to longer history-dependent dynamics. Computational overhead of mapping and the effect of mapping errors are not thoroughly studied.

Recommendations. Evaluate on established temporal graph benchmarks (e.g. social or traffic networks) and compare to state-of-the-art dynamic GNNs. Include ablations on mapping quality, multi-step versus single-step training, and edge-filter thresholds. Discuss limitations under imperfect mappings and propose ways to relax Markov assumptions (e.g. integrating recurrence). A real-world case study would greatly strengthen the practical impact.

reviews.review:
This paper introduces HIDIO, a hierarchical reinforcement learning algorithm that discovers task-agnostic latent options via a self-supervised intrinsic reward and jointly learns a scheduler to solve sparse-reward tasks. The key innovation lies in training a low-level “worker” policy to minimize conditional entropy of latent options given option-conditioned sub-trajectories, while encouraging policy entropy, and in using off-policy SAC for both levels. The authors systematically compare six sub-trajectory feature extractors, two forms of short-sighted discounting, and ablate option length. Experiments on four sparse-reward robotic tasks demonstrate that HIDIO outperforms SAC, SAC+action-repeat, HIRO, and HiPPO in sample efficiency and final success rate.

Strengths:
• Elegant unsupervised option discovery without grounding goals in state space.  
• Thorough ablations on feature extractor, discounting, and option length.  
• Strong empirical gains on both manipulation and navigation tasks with sparse rewards.  
• Clear presentation of algorithm, pseudo-code, and hyperparameter search details.

Weaknesses:
• Evaluation confined to low-dimensional state spaces; no image-based or higher-dimensional domains.  
• Sensitivity to hyperparameters (e.g., β, option dimension) is not fully characterized.  
• Reliance on hand-tuned option interval and discriminator architecture.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Extend evaluation to vision-based tasks to assess scalability with convolutional architectures.  
2. Provide sensitivity analysis of key hyperparameters (β, D, K) and guidelines for tuning.  
3. Compare to other mutual information–based skill discovery methods in a hierarchical setting.  
4. Discuss theoretical properties or failure modes when options collapse or under-explore.  
5. Report computation overhead and convergence behavior in more detail.

Overall, HIDIO presents a solid contribution to unsupervised option discovery in HRL with promising empirical results.

reviews.review:
This paper presents HIDIO, a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that discovers task-agnostic options via self-supervised intrinsic rewards and jointly learns a scheduler to maximize sparse extrinsic rewards. Key strengths include: (1) A novel worker objective that minimizes option conditional entropy on sub-trajectories while maximizing policy entropy, encouraging diverse, temporally extended behaviors without manual option grounding. (2) Joint off-policy training of both scheduler and worker using SAC, improving sample efficiency. (3) Empirical validation on four challenging sparse-reward robotic tasks (PUSHER, REACHER, GOALTASK, KICKBALL), demonstrating consistent gains over SAC, SAC+action-repeat, HIRO, and HiPPO. (4) Ablations of sub-trajectory feature extractors, short-sighted discounting, and option length provide insight into design choices.

Weaknesses and suggestions:
1. Limited domain scope: all tasks use low-dimensional MLP observations. Evaluation on vision-based or higher-dimensional environments would strengthen claims of task-agnosticism and scalability.
2. Baseline coverage: key self-supervised option methods (e.g., VALOR, DADS) and modern hierarchical approaches (e.g., DREAMER-PEARL) are not compared. Including these would clarify relative benefits of HIDIO’s discriminator designs.
3. Hyperparameter sensitivity: important factors such as entropy coefficient β and option dimension D are heuristically set. A systematic ablation over these would guide practitioners.
4. Statistical robustness: results are averaged over only three seeds. More seeds and statistical significance tests would bolster confidence in observed performance gains.
5. Theoretical insight: the paper lacks theoretical analysis of convergence or formal guarantees on option diversity. Even informal discussion of limits of conditional-entropy objectives would be valuable.
6. Clarity and reproducibility: while extensive hyperparameters are provided, the main text sometimes refers unclearly to equations and figures. A concise pseudo-code in the appendix is helpful but could be better highlighted.

Overall, HIDIO constitutes a meaningful contribution to hierarchical RL by integrating self-supervised option discovery with off-policy learning, achieving strong empirical performance. Addressing the above points would further solidify its impact.

reviews.review:
This paper introduces HIDIO, a hierarchical reinforcement learning method that discovers task-agnostic options via a self-supervised worker objective and jointly learns a scheduler to solve sparse-reward tasks. Strengths
• Novel meta-MDP formulation. The worker policy is trained to minimize the conditional entropy of latent options given sub-trajectories while maximizing its own entropy, encouraging diverse, temporally-extended behaviors without hand-designed goal spaces. • Joint option discovery and scheduling. Unlike DIAYN or VIC which pretrain skills, HIDIO integrates discovery into the hierarchy, enabling the scheduler to adapt options on–the-fly for downstream tasks. • Empirical gains. On four challenging sparse-reward robot environments, HIDIO consistently outperforms SAC, SAC+action-repeat, HIRO, and HiPPO in sample efficiency and final success, demonstrating the method’s practical benefits.
Weaknesses
• Clarity and notation. Section 3 is densely written; Eq. 5’s lower-bound derivation is terse, and the meta-MDP construction is only verbally described. Sub-scripted indices and missing equation numbers impede readability. • Limited theoretical insight. The paper lacks formal analysis of convergence or guarantees on option usefulness, making it hard to predict performance in new domains. • Hyperparameter sensitivity. Key parameters (β, option dimension D, discriminator architecture fψ) are chosen heuristically; no ablation on β or option dimensionality is reported. • Restricted benchmarks. All tasks use low-dimensional state inputs; it remains unclear whether HIDIO scales to pixel observations or more complex domains. • Missing baselines. The paper omits comparisons to end-to-end option discovery approaches (e.g. option-critic, DADS in a hierarchical setting) and fails to report discrimination accuracy.
Actionable suggestions
1. Augment Section 3 with a step-by-step derivation of Eq. 5 and provide pseudocode for the meta-MDP. 2. Include a sensitivity study over β, D, and sub-trajectory length K (beyond K={1,3,5}). 3. Add experiments on an image-based control task to validate scalability. 4. Compare to DIAYN+hierarchy and option-critic to contextualize HIDIO’s gains. 5. Report 95% confidence intervals on success rates and the discriminator’s classification accuracy.
Overall, HIDIO presents an interesting combination of self-supervised option discovery and hierarchical scheduling, but the paper would benefit from greater clarity, theoretical grounding, and broader evaluation.

reviews.review: This paper introduces HIDIO, a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that discovers task-agnostic “options” via a novel self-supervised worker objective and uses them to solve sparse-reward robotic tasks. The key idea is to train a low-level policy π_φ to minimize the conditional entropy of a latent option given its K-step sub-trajectory while maximizing its own action entropy, yielding diverse, short-term skills. A high-level scheduler π_θ then learns to sequence these skills to maximize extrinsic reward. The authors compare six sub-trajectory feature extractors, evaluate “hard” vs. “soft” shortsighted discounts, and present extensive results on two PR2 manipulation tasks (REACHER, PUSHER) and two Pioneer2DX navigation tasks (GOALTASK, KICKBALL). Strengths: 1) The worker objective is well motivated and distinguishes HIDIO from VIC/DIAYN by operating on intermediate sub-trajectories. 2) Comprehensive ablations on feature choices, K values, and pre-training vs. joint training demonstrate thorough empirical validation. 3) HIDIO outperforms SAC, SAC+ActRepeat, HIRO, and HiPPO across four sparse-reward benchmarks. 4) Qualitative visualizations of sampled options illustrate meaningful low-level skills. Weaknesses and suggestions: 1) All experiments use low-dimensional state inputs; it remains unclear how HIDIO scales to pixel observations. I suggest adding at least one vision-based environment. 2) Key hyperparameters (β, option dimension D) are set heuristically; include sensitivity analysis to assess robustness. 3) Theoretical insight into why the conditional entropy objective yields useful temporal abstractions is limited; a toy MDP or formal discussion could strengthen the contribution. 4) The scheduler and worker interaction is complex; clearer diagrams or pseudo-code in the main text would improve readability. Overall, HIDIO presents a promising, well-validated approach to self-supervised option discovery.

reviews.review:
This paper investigates the robustness of quantized neural networks—specifically binary weight and activation models—against gradient-based adversarial attacks. The authors demonstrate that quantized models suffer from gradient masking due to poor forward–backward signal propagation, leading to a false sense of security. To address this, they introduce a simple temperature scaling mechanism applied to the logits, and propose two methods for choosing the scale: Network Jacobian Scaling (NJS) and Hessian Norm Scaling (HNS). Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 across multiple architectures (ResNet, VGG, DenseNet, MobileNet) and training regimes (standard, adversarially trained) show that their PGD++ and FGSM++ attacks achieve near-100% success rates on quantized networks and outperform baseline attacks on floating-point models as well.

Strengths:
• Systematic diagnosis of gradient masking in quantized networks, grounded in empirical checks.
• Elegant, low-overhead temperature scaling methods that preserve decision boundaries.
• Comprehensive evaluation on both quantized and floating-point models, including adversarially trained networks.
• Analysis connecting singular values of the Jacobian and Hessian-based nonlinearity to attack efficacy.

Weaknesses:
• Experiments are limited to CIFAR datasets; real-world quantized models (e.g., ImageNet) are not evaluated.
• The per-sample grid search for HNS and hyperparameter ρ in NJS may incur non-negligible overhead or tuning effort.
• Theoretical justification for the optimality of the scaling methods remains empirical.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Extend evaluation to larger-scale or industry-standard quantized networks on ImageNet.
2. Report runtime and memory overhead of NJS/HNS in adversarial attacks.
3. Provide guidelines for selecting ρ and search ranges for β, or propose an automated tuning strategy.
4. Discuss whether similar scaling strategies can inform defense or quantization training for improved robustness.

reviews.review:
This paper investigates the apparent robustness of quantized neural networks (binary weight and binary weight+activation models) to gradient-based adversarial attacks. The authors demonstrate that binarized networks suffer from gradient masking—vanishing or poorly propagated gradients—leading to a false sense of security. They propose two simple yet effective temperature-scaling methods (Network Jacobian Scaling, NJS, and Hessian Norm Scaling, HNS) that rescale network logits by a single scalar to restore informative gradients without altering model decisions. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 across ResNet-18/50, VGG-16, DenseNet-121, and MobileNet-V2, including adversarially trained and floating-point baselines, show near-perfect attack success rates under L∞ and L2 bounds. The methods also outperform stronger attacks (DeepFool, BBA), black-box attacks, and variants using the Difference-of-Logits-Ratio loss, while incurring negligible overhead.

Strengths:
1. Identifies a critical gap in understanding quantized model robustness and systematically diagnoses gradient masking.
2. Proposes two principled, low-cost scaling strategies grounded in signal propagation theory.
3. Demonstrates broad empirical effectiveness across architectures, datasets, and defense settings.
4. Provides detailed derivations, ablations, and reproducibility details.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. All experiments are limited to CIFAR; extending to larger benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet quantized models) would strengthen generality.
2. Comparison to recent adaptive white-box attacks (e.g. Auto-PGD) is missing; include these baselines.
3. The choice and tuning of hyperparameters (ρ, grid search bounds) merit further discussion and guidelines.
4. Clarify computation cost for HNS grid search in practice and release code for full reproducibility.
5. Discuss limitations: cases where the Jacobian has zero singular values cannot be fixed by scaling.

Overall, the paper makes a significant contribution to understanding and attacking quantized networks and is a clear accept for ICLR.

reviews.review:
This paper presents a systematic study of gradient-based adversarial robustness in quantized neural networks (QNNs), focusing on binary networks (BNNs). The authors identify vanishing‐gradient masking—rather than genuine robustness—as the root cause of high adversarial accuracy in BNN-WQ and BNN-WAQ models. They propose two lightweight temperature-scaling attacks—Network Jacobian Scaling (NJS) and Hessian Norm Scaling (HNS)—that recondition the input gradient signal without altering the decision boundary. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 across ResNet, VGG, DenseNet and MobileNet architectures, including adversarially trained floating-point and quantized models, demonstrate near-perfect attack success rates and improvements over FGSM, PGD (L∞/L2), DeepFool, BBA, and recent Auto-PGD variants.

Strengths:
– Thorough empirical diagnosis of gradient masking in quantized networks, with multiple sanity checks.
– Simple, low‐overhead modifications (single scalar β) that generalize to both quantized and floating-point models.
– Comprehensive experimental evaluation (clean vs. perturbed accuracy, random restarts, baselines).

Weaknesses:
– Limited to small‐scale datasets (CIFAR­10/100) and full binary quantization; absence of ImageNet or multi‐bit experiments.
– Hyperparameter β selection relies on heuristic grid search; sensitivity analysis is cursory.
– Theoretical justification of the Hessian-based objective is informal; no formal robustness bounds.
– Several formatting and notation inconsistencies (equation numbering, table labels, unclear symbols).
– Missing comparisons to recent gradient‐smoothing or ensemble‐based attacks and end‐to‐end runtime overhead.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Extend evaluation to ImageNet or at least 2–4 bit quantized models to demonstrate scalability.
2. Compare against Auto-PGD with diverse loss functions (DLR) and gradient‐regularized attacks.
3. Provide a formal analysis or tighter bound on β that guarantees non-zero gradients.
4. Include runtime profiling for NJS/HNS and ablate β grid‐search range.
5. Clean up notation, fix typos in equations and tables, and release code for reproducibility.

reviews.review:
This paper investigates the robustness of quantized neural networks (QNNs) against gradient-based adversarial attacks. The authors identify that binary networks suffer from gradient masking due to poor signal propagation, leading to a false sense of security. They introduce two simple temperature-scaling methods—Network Jacobian Scaling (NJS) and Hessian Norm Scaling (HNS)—that preserve decision boundaries while amplifying input gradients. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 across ResNet, VGG, DenseNet, and MobileNet architectures demonstrate near-zero adversarial accuracy on both parameter-quantized (BNN-WQ) and weight-and-activation-quantized (BNN-WAQ) models, as well as improvements over standard PGD, FGSM, Auto-PGD with DLR loss, Square Attack, DeepFool, and BBA on floating-point and adversarially trained networks.

Strengths:
• Addresses a critical gap in understanding QNN robustness by pinpointing gradient vanishing as the root cause of fake robustness.
• Proposes methods with negligible overhead that significantly boost attack success rates.
• Validates on multiple architectures, quantization schemes, attack norms (ℓ∞/ℓ2), and compares against strong baselines.
• Provides theoretical justification linking β-scaling to Jacobian conditioning and error-signal sensitivity.

Weaknesses:
• Evaluation is limited to 1-bit quantization and CIFAR-scale datasets; generality to multi-bit quantization or ImageNet remains unclear.
• Choice of β and its practical sensitivity could be explored more systematically.
• Real hardware effects (e.g., integer rounding, non-ideal quant libraries) are not addressed.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Extend experiments to multi-bit QNNs and at least one large-scale dataset (e.g., ImageNet) to demonstrate broad applicability.
2. Include a deeper sensitivity analysis of β selection and robust default settings for practitioners.
3. Release code and pre-trained models to facilitate reproducibility and adoption by the community.
4. Investigate transfer-based and black-box attacks on QNNs to complete the robustness evaluation.

reviews.review:
This paper presents a unified optimization-based framework that interprets a wide family of convolutional graph neural networks (GCNs)—including vanilla, attention-based, and topology-based models—as solving a common Laplacian-regularized least squares problem in the graph Fourier domain. The key insight is to view different GCN filters as arising from distinct regularizers, enabling both a systematic taxonomy of existing methods and the design of new modules. As a showcase, the authors introduce a feature-variance regularizer to mitigate oversmoothing, demonstrating consistent gains on standard node classification benchmarks (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed) and on the inductive PPI task. The framework is concise, the theoretical derivations are detailed, and empirical results confirm that the proposed regularizer can be plugged into diverse architectures without retraining additional parameters.

Strengths:
• Broad unification of popular convolutional, attention, and multi-hop GNNs under a single optimization lens.
• Novel regularizer for oversmoothing that is both parameter-free and empirically effective.
• Extensive experiments across transductive and inductive settings with state-of-the-art baselines.

Weaknesses:
• The validity and error bounds of the Taylor approximations are not analyzed.
• Limited discussion of computational overhead and hyperparameter sensitivity.
• Empirical scope is confined to citation and PPI graphs; larger or more diverse tasks (e.g., graph classification) are missing.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Include theoretical bounds or empirical studies on approximation error and convergence.
2. Report runtime and memory overhead compared to baselines, and provide guidance on choosing regularization strength.
3. Extend experiments to deeper GNNs (4–8 layers) and additional datasets (e.g., OGB benchmarks).
4. Clarify and motivate derivations with intuitive illustrations in the main text.

As a showcase, the authors introduce a feature-variance regularizer to mitigate oversmoothing, demonstrating consistent gains on standard node classification benchmarks (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed) and on the inductive PPI task. The framework is concise, the theoretical derivations are detailed, and empirical results confirm that the proposed regularizer can be plugged into diverse architectures without retraining additional parameters.

Strengths:
• Broad unification of popular convolutional, attention, and multi-hop GNNs under a single optimization lens.
• Novel regularizer for oversmoothing that is both parameter-free and empirically effective.
• Extensive experiments across transductive and inductive settings with state-of-the-art baselines.

Weaknesses:
• The validity and error bounds of the Taylor approximations are not analyzed.
• Limited discussion of computational overhead and hyperparameter sensitivity.
• Empirical scope is confined to citation and PPI graphs; larger or more diverse tasks (e.g., graph classification) are missing.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Include theoretical bounds or empirical studies on approximation error and convergence.
2. Report runtime and memory overhead compared to baselines, and provide guidance on choosing regularization strength.
3. Extend experiments to deeper GNNs (4–8 layers) and additional datasets (e.g., OGB benchmarks).
4. Clarify and motivate derivations with intuitive illustrations in the main text.

reviews.review:
This paper presents a unified optimization framework for convolution‐based graph neural networks (GCNs) by casting convolutional filters as solutions to Laplacian‐regularized least squares problems in the graph Fourier domain. The authors show that vanilla GCN, residual/concatenation modules, attention‐based networks (GAT, AGNN), and topology‐based methods (PPNP, MixHop) emerge as special cases with different regularizers. As a concrete application, they introduce a feature‐variance regularizer to mitigate oversmoothing and demonstrate consistent 0.5–1.0% accuracy gains on citation and PPI datasets. Strengths:
• Originality and Scope: The paper provides a comprehensive, coherent perspective that unifies a wide range of GCN variants under a single optimization view. This synthesis deepens conceptual understanding and may guide future module design.
• Empirical Validation: Extensive experiments include transductive and inductive benchmarks, random splits, comparisons to DropEdge and PairNorm, ablations on regularization strength, iteration order, and runtime analysis, demonstrating practical benefits.
• Clarity and Reproducibility: Detailed proofs, algorithmic approximations, dataset statistics, and hyperparameter settings are provided, and the plug-in nature of the regularizer is clearly explained.
Weaknesses:
• Theoretical Guarantees: The first‐order Taylor approximations lack error bounds or convergence analysis, leaving open how closely the approximate filters match the true solutions, especially for deeper networks.
• Limited Novelty in Regularizer: While feature‐variance regularization effectively counters oversmoothing, the improvement margin is modest and its relation to existing normalization methods could be more deeply analyzed.
• Scalability and Generalization: The framework and experiments focus on small citation graphs and PPI; it remains unclear how the unified view and regularizer scale to larger graphs or other tasks (e.g., graph classification).
Actionable Suggestions:
1. Provide theoretical error bounds or empirical studies of approximation quality for the Taylor expansions at varying iteration depths.
2. Compare the feature‐variance regularizer against other oversmoothing remedies (e.g., residual depth, Jumping Knowledge) on deeper GCNs to quantify relative strengths.
3. Extend experiments to larger real‐world graphs or different tasks (e.g., structural graph benchmarks) to demonstrate scalability and broader applicability.
4. Clarify notation consistency (e.g., interplay of Θ, Θ(k), α_k) and streamline proofs to improve readability.
5. Release code and scripts to facilitate replication and adoption of the proposed framework.

reviews.review:
This paper proposes a unified optimization framework for convolution-based graph neural networks (GNNs) by interpreting existing spectral and spatial filters as solutions to regularized Laplacian least‐squares problems in the graph Fourier domain. The authors show that vanilla GCNs, attention‐based models (GAT, AGNN), topology‐based approaches (APPNP, MixHop, TAGCN), and common architectural tricks (residual connections, concatenation) can all be cast as particular choices of quadratic regularizers. As a showcase, they introduce a novel feature‐variance regularizer designed to mitigate oversmoothing, and demonstrate consistent gains (0.5–1.0%) on semi‐supervised node classification benchmarks (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed) and inductive PPI. 

Strengths:
• Comprehensive unification clarifies relationships among diverse GNN variants and architectural modules.  
• The feature‐variance regularizer is conceptually simple, plug‐in, parameter‐efficient, and yields stable improvements.  
• Empirical evaluation covers three classes of GNNs under both transductive and inductive settings, with random‐split robustness checks.  

Weaknesses:
• The theoretical derivation relies heavily on first‐order Taylor approximations and Kronecker‐product expressions; the magnitude of approximation error and convergence behavior of the iterative scheme are not analyzed.  
• The hyperparameter governing regularizer strength (α₃) appears sensitive: large values destabilize training, but no systematic ablation or guidelines are provided.  
• Computational overhead of the variance regularizer (Kronecker inverses, iterative updates) is only briefly profiled on Cora; scalability to large graphs or deeper GNNs remains unclear.  
• Experiments focus solely on node‐classification; benefits for graph‐level tasks or other modalities are untested.  

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Provide bounds on the approximation error introduced by Taylor expansions and iterative truncation.  
2. Include a more extensive hyperparameter sweep for α₃ and report sensitivity curves.  
3. Benchmark runtime and memory overhead on larger graphs (e.g. OGB datasets) and with deeper architectures (8+ layers).  
4. Extend experiments to graph‐classification/regression benchmarks to demonstrate broader applicability.  
5. Clarify notation in Section 3 and move some lengthy derivations from Appendix to the main text for readability.

Overall, while the unification framework is conceptually appealing and the oversmoothing remedy shows promise, the paper lacks rigorous analysis of approximation errors, scalability, and broader empirical validation. I recommend borderline rejection and encourage a revision addressing the above points.

reviews.review:
This paper presents a unified optimization-based framework that casts various graph convolutional network (GCN) variants as instances of a Laplacian-regularized least-squares problem in the graph Fourier domain, and proposes a novel feature-variance regularizer to mitigate oversmoothing. Strengths: 1. The framework offers a clear, unifying mathematical interpretation of vanilla GCN, attention-based GCNs, and topology-based methods such as PPNP and MixHop through carefully chosen regularizers. 2. The proposed feature-variance regularizer is well motivated and addresses oversmoothing by explicitly penalizing collapse of feature dimensions. 3. Empirical results on Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, and PPI show consistent, modest gains (0.5–1.0% accuracy) over strong baselines, including comparison to DropEdge and PairNorm.

Weaknesses: 1. Novelty is limited: related work in graph signal processing (e.g., Hoang & Maehara 2019; Zhao & Akoglu 2019) already interprets GCNs as low-pass filters, and the unified formulation mainly assembles prior observations. 2. The reliance on first-order Taylor approximations weakens the theoretical claims, but no error bounds are provided. 3. Experiments focus on small citation graphs; scaling behavior and runtime overhead of the Kronecker-based regularizer (even with iterative approximation) are not analyzed. 4. Some key baselines (e.g., DropEdge, PPNP variants) are only briefly touched; an ablation on approximation order and sensitivity to hyperparameters is missing.

Actionable suggestions: 1. Provide theoretical guarantees or empirical analysis of approximation error introduced by the Taylor expansion. 2. Include runtime and memory cost comparisons on larger graphs. 3. Expand experiments to other domains (e.g., heterophilous graphs or link prediction). 4. Clarify novelty relative to existing graph signal processing interpretations and position this work accordingly. 5. Release code and hyperparameter settings for reproducibility.

This paper addresses the important problem of learning deep latent variable models in missing-not-at-random (MNAR) settings by introducing the not-MIWAE, a principled extension of the MIWAE framework. The key idea is to jointly model data and missingness via a parametrized mask distribution pφ(s|x), and to perform importance-weighted variational inference with reparameterization in both latent and data spaces. Strengths:
- Novelty: Extends deep generative modeling to MNAR data, filling a gap in current VAE/IWAE approaches.
- Technical soundness: The derivation of the lower bound and its gradient estimator is clear and builds on established IWAE theory; convergence properties are well discussed.
- Empirical validation: Demonstrates consistent improvements over MIWAE, PPCA, and state-of-the-art MNAR and propensity-based methods on UCI tabular benchmarks, SVHN clipping experiments, and the Yahoo! R3 recommender task.
Weaknesses:
- The approach assumes reparameterizable observation models, limiting direct applicability to discrete or complex distributions.
- Theoretical identifiability under general MNAR is not fully addressed; recovery guarantees are left for future work.
- Learning the mask model can be unstable without strong prior knowledge, suggesting a delicate trade-off between data and missingness model complexity.
Actionable suggestions:
1. Explore continuous relaxations or alternative gradient estimators to support categorical/discrete data.
2. Provide an ablation study on sensitivity to missing-model architecture and misspecification.
3. Investigate conditions under which the joint model is identifiable, possibly leveraging recent MNAR recoverability results.
Overall, this is a solid contribution that meaningfully advances deep generative modeling for MNAR data.

reviews.review:
Summary:
The manuscript introduces not-MIWAE, an extension of the MIWAE framework to handle data missing not at random (MNAR) by explicitly modelling the missingness mechanism pφ(s|x) alongside a deep latent variable model pθ(x|z). The authors derive an importance-weighted variational bound that integrates over both latent codes and missing values, using the reparameterization trick in data space. Experiments on synthetic PPCA, UCI benchmarks with self-masking, clipped SVHN images, and the Yahoo! R3 recommender dataset demonstrate consistent gains in test log-likelihood and imputation error over MAR baselines, low-rank joint models, propensity-scoring methods, and alternative deep models.

Strengths:
• Addresses the underexplored MNAR setting in deep generative modelling, moving beyond MAR/MCAR assumptions.  
• Elegant variational inference scheme, reusing IWAE theory and applying reparameterization to data samples.  
• Comprehensive empirical evaluation across diverse domains, including high-dimensional images and real-world recommendation data.  
• Demonstrates clear performance gains over strong baselines (MIWAE, propensity-corrected MF, HI-VAE etc.).

Weaknesses:
• Identifiability: no discussion of when the full data distribution can be recovered under MNAR; missing models may be unidentifiable.  
• Ablations lacking: effect of missing-model architecture, number of importance samples, latent dimension, or mis-specification of pφ(s|x).  
• Limited complexity in missing-model networks (only logistic/agnostic layers) and single-channel SVHN; scalability to more complex image categories is unclear.  
• Reproducibility: key hyperparameters and training schedules only in appendix; clarity could be improved.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Include a brief theoretical discussion on identifiability and cite recent MNAR recoverability work (e.g. Mohan & Pearl 2021, Sadinle & Reiter 2018).  
2. Add ablation studies on the missing-model complexity, importance sample count K, and latent dimension to assess robustness.  
3. Clarify all architectural and optimization settings in the main text or an organized supplement.  
4. Experiment with a deeper missing-model network or additional image datasets to test scalability.  

Overall, this work makes a significant contribution to deep generative modelling under non-ignorable missingness. I recommend acceptance.

reviews.review:
This paper addresses the important problem of training deep latent variable models when data are Missing Not At Random (MNAR). The proposed not-MIWAE extends the IWAE framework by jointly modelling the data and missingness mechanism with a neural network for p(s|x), and uses reparameterisation in both latent and data spaces. The authors present a clear derivation of the bound, discuss identifiability issues, and demonstrate empirical gains over MAR baselines on PPCA, UCI regression, SVHN images, and the Yahoo! R3 recommendation dataset. Strengths:
 • Novelty: Explicit neural modelling of MNAR missingness within importance-weighted variational inference is original and well motivated.
 • Breadth of experiments: From toy PPCA to real-world image clipping and recommender biases, the method consistently improves imputation and likelihood.
 • Flexibility: The missing model can incorporate domain priors (e.g. self-masking, known sign), and the framework admits both continuous and discrete data.
 Weaknesses:
 • Limited theoretical guidance on identifiability: While the bound converges, it remains unclear under which conditions (beyond citation of Mohan & Pearl) the true data distribution can be recovered.
 • Missing ablations: The trade-off between decoder complexity and missing model flexibility deserves systematic exploration (e.g. how performance varies with latent dimension or missing model capacity).
 • Computational cost: Joint training with data-space reparameterisation is expensive; wall-clock comparisons or scaling analysis are absent.
 • Missing baselines: Recent MNAR or debiasing imputation methods such as HI-VAE, p-VAE, GAIN or Sportisse et al. are mentioned but not empirically compared.
 Actionable suggestions:
 1. Add ablation studies on latent dimension, number of importance samples, and missing model capacity.
 2. Include runtime/memory profiling versus MIWAE to quantify overhead.
 3. Provide more theory on identifiability (even under simplified missing models).
 4. Compare to at least one recent MNAR baseline (e.g. GAIN or HI-VAE) on a common dataset.

reviews.review:
This paper addresses the challenging problem of training deep latent variable models when data are missing not at random (MNAR). The authors propose the not-MIWAE, which extends the importance-weighted autoencoder framework by jointly modeling the missingness mechanism with a flexible neural network. The key innovation is to incorporate a missing data model p_{φ}(s|x) into a variational inference scheme and to apply reparameterization in both latent and data spaces. Experiments on synthetic PPCA, UCI self-masking benchmarks, clipped SVHN images, and the Yahoo! R3 recommender dataset demonstrate improved test log-likelihoods and imputation errors relative to MAR-based baselines and recent MNAR methods.

Strengths:
• Novel joint DLVM architecture for MNAR that subsumes MIWAE and enables encoding prior knowledge on missingness.  
• Rigorous derivation of a multi-sample lower bound and efficient gradient estimation via reparameterization.  
• Broad empirical evaluation across diverse modalities, showing consistent gains in imputation RMSE and likelihood.

Weaknesses:
• Identifiability concerns: the joint model may not recover true data distribution without strong missingness priors.  
• Missing-model mis-specification is not fully studied; performance may degrade if the assumed mechanism is wrong.  
• Limited sensitivity analysis on number of importance samples K, network depth, or failure modes.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Include an ablation on the impact of misspecified or overly flexible missing models, quantifying robustness.  
2. Provide theoretical or empirical identifiability conditions under which not-MIWAE recovers the true joint distribution.  
3. Compare against additional recent MNAR methods (e.g., Collier et al., 2020), and explore discrete observation models without continuous relaxations.

reviews.review:
This paper presents a principled extension of model-prediction-error intrinsic rewards to a multi-model setting, introducing an adaptive fusion rule based on the α-mean and meta-gradient optimization of α. Strengths:
• Novelty: The use of multiple probabilistic transition models combined via a scale-free α-mean is a fresh approach that generalizes single-model curiosity and disagreement methods. The theoretical justification via information geometry is solid.
• Technical soundness: The derivation of the α-mean as the unique scale-free fusion satisfying monotonic adaptation is rigorous. The meta-gradient scheme for updating α alongside policy parameters is well motivated.
• Empirical evaluation: Comprehensive experiments on six sparse-reward continuous control benchmarks (MuJoCo) show consistent gains over strong baselines (curiosity, hashing, surprise, disagreement, intrinsic reward module). Ablations on static fusion rules, model order K, and α dynamics convincingly demonstrate the benefit of adaptive fusion.
Weaknesses:
• Computational overhead: Training K probabilistic models and computing meta-gradients for α adds cost. A runtime or sample-efficiency comparison would clarify trade-offs.
• Hyperparameter sensitivity: Performance depends on K, reg. weight, and learning rates for α. A sensitivity study would strengthen reproducibility.
• Limited domains: Experiments are restricted to continuous locomotion tasks. It is unclear how the method generalizes to discrete or higher-dimensional state spaces.
Actionable suggestions:
1) Include wall-clock time and sample-efficiency plots comparing overhead to baselines.
2) Provide a hyperparameter sensitivity analysis for K, α initialization, and regularization weight.
3) Test on at least one discrete sparse-reward task (e.g., gridworld) to show broader applicability.
4) Clarify key derivations (Appendix G) and make code publicly available.
Overall, this is a well-executed contribution that advances intrinsic motivation in sparse-reward RL and merits acceptance.

reviews.review:
This paper addresses sparse‐reward reinforcement learning by generalizing the single‐model prediction‐error intrinsic reward to a multi‐model setting, and proposes an adaptive fusion of K model errors via the α‐mean from information geometry. The fusion weight α is meta‐learned by optimizing the extrinsic return through a meta‐gradient step per policy update. The method is instantiated under PPO with Gaussian mixture transition models, and evaluated on six MuJoCo locomotion tasks in a delayed‐reward sparse setting. Ablations study the evolution of α, the effect of static vs. learned fusion, and model order K. Results show consistent improvements over five established intrinsic‐reward baselines (curiosity, surprise, hashing, information gain, disagreement) and Zheng et al.’s learned‐module approach.

Strengths:
• Novel fusion principle: Applying the α‐mean yields a unified, scale‐free interpolation between min/mean/max fusions, supported by a uniqueness proof.  
• Principled meta‐optimization: Jointly learning α via meta‐gradients aligns intrinsic reward fusion with downstream policy performance.

reviews.review:
This paper addresses intrinsic reward design in sparse-reward RL by combining multiple environment models via an adaptive fusion driven by information-geometric α-means and meta-gradient learning of α. Strengths include: the clear motivation to exploit diversity from multiple prediction models; a principled derivation of the α-mean as the unique scale-free fusion satisfying natural conditions; a meta-gradient scheme that successfully adapts α over training, recovering aggressive exploration early and conservative fusion later; thorough ablations demonstrating the effect of K, static vs adaptive fusion, and fusion versus NN-based alternatives; and strong empirical gains on six Mujoco sparse-reward locomotion tasks compared to five prior intrinsic-reward methods under matched PPO baselines.  

Weaknesses and concerns:  
1. Incremental novelty—while α-mean fusion is elegant, prior work (Pathak et al. 2019, Zheng et al. 2018) also exploit multi-model disagreement or learned intrinsic modules; the paper could better distinguish from or integrate those methods.  
2. Computational cost and sensitivity—the overhead of K models and meta-gradient updates is not reported; wall-clock time, memory footprint, and stability across random seeds merit discussion.  
3. Limited scope—the evaluation is restricted to continuous locomotion; it is unclear whether adaptive fusion helps in discrete tasks, high-dimensional vision inputs, or non-delayed sparse rewards.  
4. Presentation issues—conversion artifacts ("cid" tokens), inconsistent equation numbering, and unclear hyperparameter selection distract from readability.  
5. Reproducibility—code availability, pseudo-code clarity (e.g. mixing coefficient updates), and specific seed splits are needed.  

Actionable suggestions:  
- Provide runtime/memory comparison and sensitivity analysis over K and learning rates.  
- Extend experiments to at least one non-locomotion sparse task (e.g. navigation).  
- Clean up notation, ensure a self-contained derivation, and release code with exact hyperparameters.  

Overall, the work is solid but would benefit from clearer positioning, broader evaluation, and improved presentation.

reviews.review:
Summary
The authors propose an intrinsic-reward framework for sparse-reward reinforcement learning that generalizes single-model prediction-error methods to multiple models. They fuse K model-prediction errors via an α-mean from information geometry, and learn the fusion parameter α online using meta-gradient optimization. Experiments on six delayed-reward MuJoCo tasks demonstrate consistent gains over single-model surprise, hashing, curiosity, disagreement, information-gain, and an intrinsic-reward module baseline.
Strengths
• Novel fusion: The use of the α-mean provides a principled, scale-free fusion family covering min, max, and average, and meta-gradient learning of α adapts exploration vs. exploitation automatically.  
• Empirical rigor: Evaluation across six continuous control benchmarks with sparse rewards, ten seeds, and ablations on α dynamics, number of models K, and error measures (KLD vs. MSE) builds confidence in robustness.  
• Insightful analysis: The α schedule aligns with exploration intuition, and comparisons to learned neural-net fusion highlight the efficiency of the single-parameter α approach.
Weaknesses
• Computational cost: The meta-gradient updates and second-order optimization for K probabilistic models add overhead; runtime and sample-efficiency trade-offs are not quantified.  
• Baseline scope: Recent exploration methods such as Random Network Distillation or RIDE are omitted; comparisons focus on first-generation methods.  
• Task diversity: Results are limited to continuous MuJoCo with artificial delay-based sparsity; discrete or real-world sparse domains are not addressed.
Actionable Suggestions
1) Report wall-clock training time, memory, and complexity vs. single-model baselines.  
2) Include comparisons to stronger intrinsic-exploration methods (e.g. RND, RIDE) and off-policy learners.  
3) Evaluate on additional sparse tasks (discrete control or vision-based benchmarks) to demonstrate broader applicability.  
4) Analyze sensitivity to K, α initialization, and learning rates, and discuss stability of meta-gradient updates.

reviews.review:
This paper addresses an important gap between the theory and practice of adaptive gradient methods by studying their convergence in the over-parameterized (interpolation) setting. Under smooth convex interpolation, the authors prove that constant-step AMSGrad achieves an O(1/T) rate and that AdaGrad similarly converges at this faster rate when interpolation is exact. They further show that when interpolation is only approximate, both methods still converge to a small neighborhood of the optimum, and AdaGrad remains robust to interpolation violation. To remove the need for tuning, the paper integrates stochastic line-search (SLS) and Polyak step-sizes (SPS) into AdaGrad and AMSGrad, proving that these variants adapt to local smoothness without prior knowledge of Lipschitz constants while retaining convergence guarantees. The empirical evaluation is extensive, spanning synthetic convex problems, RBF kernel classification, deep matrix factorization, and image classification on CIFAR and ImageNet variants. Across tasks, AdaGrad/AMSGrad with SLS consistently outperforms tuned SGD, Adam, and recent Adam variants, often improving both convergence speed and generalization.

Weaknesses include: (1) the theoretical analysis is limited to smooth convex objectives and does not extend to non-convex deep networks; (2) the worst-case constants in the convergence bounds are loose, leaving a gap to SGD rates; (3) the momentum analysis yields no theoretical benefit and imposes restrictive parameter ranges; (4) line-search overhead and hyperparameter sensitivity (e.g., choice of c) warrant further discussion. 

To strengthen the work, I suggest: (i) outlining a path toward non-convex convergence analysis under interpolation; (ii) tightening constants or providing data-dependent bounds; (iii) adding an ablation on line-search cost vs. gains; and (iv) exploring alternative momentum formulations that yield provable benefits.

reviews.review:
This paper studies adaptive gradient methods in the interpolation regime, establishing O(1/T) convergence for constant–step–size AMSGrad (with momentum) and AdaGrad under smooth, convex interpolation. To alleviate step–size tuning in practice, it integrates stochastic line–search (SLS) and Polyak step–sizes (SPS) into AdaGrad/AMSGrad, proving that these variants match the constant–step–size rates without requiring knowledge of Lipschitz constants. Empirically, the authors demonstrate improved convergence and generalization on binary classification, deep networks (CIFAR, Tiny ImageNet), kernels, and deep matrix factorization.
Strengths:
• Theoretical contributions reconcile practice (constant step–sizes, fixed momentum) with rigorous convergence guarantees in the over-parameterized, convex setting.
• Novel combination of adaptive preconditioners with stochastic line–search and SPS, with matching rates and step–size adaptivity.
• Extensive experiments across convex and non-convex tasks showing both speed and test accuracy gains.
• Solid engagement with recent interpolation literature and line–search methods.
Weaknesses and suggestions:
1. Scope of theory is limited to smooth, convex interpolation; a non-convex analysis is missing. Extending or discussing obstacles toward non-convex guarantees (e.g. neural networks) would strengthen impact.
2. Bounds involve large constants (dependence on d, amax/amin); tighter analysis or discussion of whether these constants affect practice would improve clarity.
3. The “conservative” line–search resets (γ, τ parameters) require tuning; include ablation on sensitivity to c, γ to guide practitioners.
4. Clarify the bounded iterates assumption and its necessity versus projection steps.
5. Provide code release and detailed hyperparameter settings (batch sizes, backtracking schedule) to ensure reproducibility.
Overall, this work offers valuable theoretical and practical insights into adaptive methods under interpolation and is recommended for acceptance.

reviews.review:
This paper investigates convergence of AdaGrad and AMSGrad under interpolation, proving O(1/T) rates for smooth, convex objectives, and introduces stochastic line-search (SLS) and Polyak step-size (SPS) to adaptively set learning rates without tuning. Extensive experiments on synthetic separable data, kernel SVM, and deep nets (CIFAR, Tiny ImageNet) demonstrate that AdaGrad/AMSGrad with SLS/SPS outperform tuned Adam, RAdam, AdaBound, and SGD with SLS.

Strengths:
• The theoretical analysis leverages interpolation to close gaps between practice (constant step-sizes, fixed momentum) and existing theory.  
• Novel application of stochastic Armijo and Polyak line-search to adaptive methods, with convergence proofs matching constant-step guarantees.  
• Broad empirical evaluation across convex and non-convex tasks confirms practical benefit of step-size adaptation.

Weaknesses:
• Heavy reliance on strong assumptions (convexity, smoothness, bounded iterates, bounded preconditioners) limits applicability to non-convex deep learning.  
• Theoretical constants depend poorly on dimension d, smoothness Lmax, and eigenvalue bounds amin, making rates vacuous in high dimensions.  
• SLS incurs additional forward/backward overhead; wall-clock trade-offs are only partially characterized.  
• Key hyperparameter choices (backtracking factor, c values, momentum β) lack systematic tuning or justification.  
• Experiments omit error bars, seed variability, and ablations on reset heuristics and SPS smoothing.

Suggestions:
1. Include controlled convex experiments where theoretical constants can be measured and compared to empirical rates.  
2. Provide detailed ablations on line-search parameters (c, backtracking, reset strategies) and report standard deviations over multiple runs.  
3. Analyze wall-clock vs iteration trade-off more rigorously, perhaps via FLOP counting.  
4. Extend discussion to non-convex proof extensions or show practical heuristics for non-decreasing momentum.  
5. Open-source code with clear hyperparameter settings to facilitate reproducibility.

Overall, the paper offers valuable theoretical insights and practical techniques, but the accumulation of strong assumptions, loose constants, and insufficient ablations suggests it is marginally below the acceptance threshold.

reviews.review:
This paper investigates the convergence behavior of two popular adaptive optimizers, AdaGrad and AMSGrad, under the interpolation regime for smooth, convex objectives. The authors prove that both methods achieve an accelerated O(1/T) convergence rate when the interpolation condition holds exactly, and characterize their convergence to a neighborhood when interpolation is violated. To remove the need for tuning problem-dependent step-size parameters, they introduce stochastic line-search (SLS) and stochastic Polyak step-sizes (SPS) tailored to adaptive methods, and prove that these variants retain comparable convergence guarantees. Extensive experiments on binary classification, kernel methods, and deep neural networks demonstrate that AdaGrad and AMSGrad equipped with SLS/SPS consistently match or outperform tuned Adam, SGD with line search, and recent Adam variants, both in training speed and generalization.

Strengths:
• Theoretical contribution: Extends interpolation analysis from SGD to adaptive methods, filling a notable gap. 
• Rigorous proofs: Clear statements of assumptions and convergence bounds. 
• Practical impact: SLS/SPS variants eliminate manual step-size tuning and improve empirical performance across tasks. 
• Broad evaluation: Benchmarks on convex, non-convex, kernel, and deep settings.

Weaknesses:
• Theory confined to convex, smooth objectives; non-convex experiments lack corresponding guarantees. 
• Hyperparameter sensitivity of SLS/SPS (e.g. choice of c, backtracking schedule) is not fully explored. 
• Line-search adds per-iteration overhead; impact on large-scale training warrants further study.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Extend or discuss prospects for non-convex interpolation analysis to better align theory with deep-net experiments. 
2. Include ablations on SLS/SPS hyperparameters (c, backtracking factors) to assess robustness. 
3. Report wall-clock vs. epoch trade-offs for line-search overhead on large models. 
4. Compare against alternative adaptive step-size schemes (e.g. L4).

reviews.review:
This paper addresses the challenge of scaling marginalized importance sampling (MIS) for off-policy evaluation (OPE) to high-dimensional domains by leveraging the successor representation (SR) of the target policy. The authors derive SR-DICE, a method that recasts density-ratio estimation as reward‐function optimization, decoupling environment dynamics from ratio estimation. This yields a simple convex loss for a linear weight vector w after pretraining an encoder and deep SR via standard TD methods. A case study recasts DualDICE as a Bellman‐residual minimization, illuminating its optimization pathologies. Empirically on Atari and MuJoCo tasks, SR-DICE outperforms recent MIS baselines (DualDICE, GradientDICE) in stability and accuracy, though direct deep TD evaluation still achieves lower OPE error.

Strengths:
• Novel insight: relating MIS to SR and reward‐function optimization, simplifying ratio estimation.
• Scalable algorithm: leverages off-the-shelf deep RL for SR, no minimax optimization.
• Empirical evaluation: thorough benchmarks on challenging high-dimensional tasks.
• Theoretical connections: clear derivations and reward-shaping view of DualDICE.

Weaknesses:
• For OPE, SR-DICE still underperforms deep TD, limiting practical impact on evaluation.
• Baselines are restricted to two MIS methods; comparison with Fitted Q Evaluation or weighted IS is missing.
• Sensitivity to SR approximation quality is acknowledged but not deeply characterized.

Suggestions:
• Include additional OPE baselines (FQE, MRDR) and classical IS methods for broader context.
• Provide empirical studies on SR error propagation and w estimation robustness.
• Highlight scenarios where accurate density ratios (rather than value estimates) are crucial.
• Discuss potential extensions to off-policy learning or imitation uses of the ratios.

Overall, SR-DICE is a well‐motivated, technically sound contribution that advances understanding of MIS and SR. The insights and code release will benefit future work on density-ratio estimation and its downstream uses.

reviews.review:
This paper presents SR-DICE, a novel marginalized importance sampling (MIS) algorithm that leverages the successor representation (SR) to estimate density ratios for off-policy evaluation (OPE). By shifting the difficulty of density‐ratio estimation onto SR learning, the method avoids complex minimax objectives and scales to high-dimensional continuous and image-based domains. Empirical evaluation on seven MuJoCo tasks (Pendulum through Humanoid) and five Atari games shows SR-DICE yields substantially lower MSE than DualDICE and GradientDICE. The paper also provides a theoretical reward-optimization perspective linking MIS to Bellman residual minimization, illuminating the instability of previous methods.

Strengths:
• Novel insight: reframing MIS as reward learning and computing ratios via SR is both simple and elegant.
• Algorithmic contribution: SR-DICE requires only convex regression once SR is learned, avoiding double-sampling and adversarial optimization.
• Empirical breadth: benchmarks on both continuous control and Atari, including ablations on data size, discount, behavior noise, and representations.
• Analysis: the case study on DualDICE via value-function perspective elucidates why it overestimates.
• Reproducibility: code and hyperparameters are provided in appendices.

Weaknesses:
• Baselines: comparison omits popular OPE approaches such as Fitted Q-Evaluation, doubly-robust estimators or MAGIC, which may further contextualize performance.
• SR approximation error: theoretical guarantees assume exact SR; practical sensitivity to representation errors is not deeply explored.
• Hyperparameter sensitivity: undisclosed tuning procedures leave open how robust SR-DICE is across architectures.
• Practical impact: since deep TD and direct SR match or exceed SR-DICE, concrete OPE advantages of MIS remain limited.

Suggestions:
1. Include additional baselines (FQE, DR, MAGIC) to clarify where SR-DICE sits relative to mainstream OPE.
2. Analyze how SR approximation error propagates to ratio estimation and OPE bias/variance.
3. Report sensitivity to key hyperparameters (learning rates, network sizes) to strengthen reproducibility.
4. Discuss computational complexity and wall-clock time relative to baselines.  

Overall, SR-DICE offers a compelling simplification and new perspective on MIS, but its practical OPE gains relative to value-based methods require further demonstration.

reviews.review:
This paper introduces SR-DICE, a practical algorithm for marginalized importance sampling (MIS) via the successor representation (SR) of a target policy. By reframing density‐ratio estimation as reward‐function optimization over SR features, the authors derive a simple convex loss for the ratio estimator and leverage deep RL methods to learn the SR in high‐dimensional domains. They show that SR‐DICE outperforms recent MIS baselines (DualDICE, GradientDICE) on MuJoCo and Atari OPE benchmarks and highlight connections between MIS and Bellman residual minimization.

Strengths:
– Novel insight: MIS ratios can be obtained via SR, decoupling dynamics learning from ratio optimization.  
– Simple convex optimization for w, avoiding unstable minimax training.  
– Extensive empirical evaluation demonstrating stability and scalability of SR‐DICE vs MIS baselines.  
– Clear derivation connecting DualDICE to Bellman residuals, yielding useful intuition.

Weaknesses:
– OPE performance still trails (and is roughly matched by) direct deep‐TD and deep‐SR baselines, limiting practical OPE impact.  
– No evaluation of density‐ratio applications (e.g., off‐policy gradient, imitation), which would better justify SR‐DICE beyond OPE.  
– Approximation error from learned φ and ψ is neither theoretically bounded nor empirically analyzed.  
– Hyperparameter sensitivity (e.g., target‐network update, φ‐loss weights) is not studied.  
– Notational inconsistencies (e.g., use of dπ/dD without explicit sampling assumptions) and minor typos distract.

Actionable suggestions:
1. Empirically evaluate SR‐DICE density ratios in a downstream task (off‐policy policy gradient or imitation).  
2. Report estimation bias/variance of w(s,a) vs baselines and analyze convergence rates.  
3. Include ablations on SR pre‐training horizon, target‐network update frequency, and φ‐loss weights.  
4. Tighten theoretical discussion by characterizing how SR approximation error impacts w.  
5. Clean up notation, define all symbols before use, and fix minor formatting errors.

While the SR‐based perspective is interesting, the paper’s practical contributions remain incremental for OPE alone. I recommend rejection pending stronger downstream validation and more thorough error analysis.

reviews.review: This paper introduces SR-DICE, a practical algorithm for marginalized importance sampling (MIS) in off-policy evaluation (OPE) that leverages the successor representation (SR) to decouple dynamics learning from density-ratio estimation. The key insight is to recast MIS as reward-function optimization and recover density ratios via a simple convex least-squares problem over SR features. The authors derive a connection between existing MIS methods (e.g. DualDICE) and Bellman residual minimization, offering fresh intuition about their optimization challenges. Empirical validation spans high-dimensional MuJoCo and Atari tasks, demonstrating that SR-DICE substantially outperforms prior MIS baselines in both stability and accuracy.   Strengths:  • Novel derivation linking successor representations to MIS, yielding a convex, single-phase optimization.  • Comprehensive empirical study across seven continuous-control and multiple Atari benchmarks, including ablations on data size, discount factor, and behavior policies.  • Insightful case study on DualDICE’s implicit Bellman residual, clarifying why minimax methods struggle.  • Public code release promoting reproducibility.   Weaknesses:  • Novelty is moderate: SR and MIS are established concepts, and their combination yields a straightforward least-squares solution.  • The main empirical conclusion—that deep TD outperforms all MIS methods—limits practical impact for OPE.  • Theoretical analysis lacks error bounds under approximate SR.  • Applications beyond OPE (e.g., imitation, policy regularization) are suggested but not demonstrated.   Suggestions:  1. Provide theoretical guarantees or bounds relating SR approximation error to density-ratio estimation error. 2. Experiment with end-to-end joint training of SR and weights to reduce pretraining overhead. 3. Illustrate SR-DICE’s utility in a downstream task (e.g., offline policy optimization or imitation learning).  4. Clarify derivation steps in Section 3.1 and streamline notation for readability.
