reviews.review:
This paper proposes “diff pruning,” a sparse, parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that represents task adaptation as a sparse delta vector added to frozen pretrained parameters. The key innovation is a differentiable L0-norm relaxation to learn the mask and weights of this delta vector, combined with a post-training L0-ball projection (magnitude pruning) and optional structured grouping. Experiments on BERTLARGE across GLUE and SQuAD v1.1 demonstrate that diff pruning (0.5–1% added parameters per task) matches or exceeds full fine-tuning and adapter baselines (which require 3.6–8% parameters), while structured sparsity further improves performance.

Strengths:
• Novel formulation: Recasting fine-tuning as learning an additive sparse diff is conceptually elegant and unifies parameter-efficient transfer with L0 regularization techniques.
• Strong empirical results: Comprehensive GLUE and SQuAD evaluations show competitive or superior performance at drastically lower parameter budgets.
• Structured pruning variant: Incorporating group masks yields better sparsity/performance tradeoffs and interpretable layer-wise adaptation patterns.
• Clarity and reproducibility: The technical derivation is detailed, and hyperparameter settings are clearly described.

Weaknesses:
• Training overhead: The cost of learning mask parameters increases memory and runtime (1.5×–2×), but concrete benchmarks or mitigations are lacking.
• Hyperparameter sensitivity: The choice of λ, l, r, and mask initialization may not generalize; an ablation on λ and stretching bounds would strengthen the claims.
• Limited scope: Evaluation is restricted to English NLP tasks and BERT; assessing other architectures or non-text domains would broaden impact.

Actionable suggestions:
1. Include a runtime/memory breakdown versus full fine-tuning and adapters.
2. Provide ablations on λ, l, r, and mask initialization to establish robustness.
3. Evaluate on additional domains (e.g., vision Transformers or multilingual tasks) to demonstrate wider applicability.
4. Release code and pretrained diff masks to facilitate adoption.

Overall, diff pruning is a significant step toward ultra-lightweight task adaptation and merits acceptance.

reviews.review:
This paper addresses parameter-efficient transfer learning by representing task-specific fine-tuning as learning a sparse “diff” vector added to frozen pretrained weights. A continuous relaxation of the L₀ norm (Hard-Concrete) is used to learn a binary mask over the diff vector, and an L₀-ball projection via magnitude pruning guarantees an exact sparsity budget. Experiments on GLUE and SQuAD with BERT_LARGE show that diff pruning matches or exceeds full fine-tuning and adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019) while modifying only 0.5–1% of parameters per task. The structured variant, which masks groups of parameters, further improves performance and yields interpretable task-specific adaptation patterns.

Strengths:
• Novel formulation: viewing fine-tuning as learning a sparse diff vector is simple yet effective and generalizes beyond adapters. 
• Strong empirical results: matches or outperforms adapters across 9 GLUE tasks and SQuAD with substantially fewer parameters.
• Detailed analysis: ablations on sparsity budgets, structured vs. unstructured masks, layerwise adaptation patterns, and SQuAD extension.
• Clear exposition: mathematical derivations, training details, and hyperparameters are described.

Weaknesses & Suggestions:
• Limited baselines: recent parameter-efficient methods such as prefix-tuning, LoRA (Hu et al., 2021) or bit-level distillation are not compared. I recommend including these baselines or discussing distinctions.
• Hyperparameter sensitivity: the choice of λ, l, r, and initialization of α and w is fixed; an ablation on these values and variability across random seeds would strengthen reproducibility.
• Computational and memory overhead: training is 1.5–2× slower and uses more GPU memory. Quantify these costs and discuss trade-offs relative to adapters.
• Scope of evaluation: experiments are limited to English BERT_LARGE. Testing on other architectures (e.g. T5, RoBERTa) or modalities (vision) would demonstrate broader applicability.
• Code release & reproducibility: while many details are given, publishing code, seed settings, and exact data preprocessing pipelines is crucial for replicability.

Overall, the method is well motivated, empirically validated, and offers a practical alternative to adapters for low-resource deployment. With broader baselines and cost analyses, this work will make a strong contribution to parameter-efficient transfer learning.

reviews.review:
Summary:
This paper introduces “diff pruning,” a parameter-efficient finetuning method that learns sparse task-specific difference vectors added to a fixed pretrained parameter vector. Using a differentiable L0 relaxation and optional structured grouping, the method stores only nonzero diffs per task and matches full BERTLARGE performance on GLUE (0.5% of parameters) and SQuAD (1%) while outperforming nonadaptive pruning baselines and narrowing the gap to adapters (3–4% parameter overhead).

Strengths:
• Novel framing: Viewing finetuning as learning sparse diffs on top of a shared model is intuitive and generalizable. 
• Technical soundness: The L0‐norm relaxation via hard concrete and group variants is well motivated and builds on prior work. 
• Empirical performance: Matching full finetuning on GLUE and SQuAD with 0.5–1% additional parameters is impressive. Structured diff pruning yields consistent gains over nonstructured and magnitude‐only baselines. 
• Analysis: Layer‐wise sparsity patterns and task‐specific distributions provide valuable insights.

Weaknesses:
• Limited baselines: The study omits recent adapter variants (e.g., Pfeiffer et al.’s MAD-X) and other PETL methods (prefix tuning), hindering direct comparison. 
• Efficiency metrics: Training time, GPU memory overhead (1.5–2× slower), and inference speed are underreported. 
• Hyperparameter sensitivity: The choice of λ, l, r, α initialization lacks ablation; it is unclear how robust the method is across varied settings or tasks beyond GLUE/SQuAD. 
• Formatting inconsistencies: Numerous “(cid:…)” artifacts, footnote placement issues, and reference numbering errors distract from the presentation.

Suggestions for improvement:
1. Add comparisons to state-of-the-art PETL methods (MAD-X, prefix tuning) and report inference latency/memory usage. 2. Include ablations for λ, hard‐concrete bounds, and initialization to demonstrate robustness. 3. Clean up formatting artifacts and proofread equations and footnotes. 4. Consider extending evaluation to additional tasks (e.g., multilingual, few‐shot).

Overall, diff pruning is a valuable contribution to parameter‐efficient transfer learning, but the empirical and presentation gaps warrant minor improvements before publication.

reviews.review:
Summary: This paper introduces diff pruning, a method for parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large pretrained models by learning and sparsely pruning a task-specific “diff” vector added to fixed pretrained weights. Empirical studies on GLUE and SQuAD demonstrate that diff pruning matches full fine-tuning performance while modifying as little as 0.5–1% of parameters, and a structured variant further improves sparsity/performance tradeoffs.

Strengths:
- Clear motivation: addresses the growing storage and memory burden of multi-task fine-tuning.
- Elegant formulation: reparameterizes fine-tuning as learning a sparse diff vector with a differentiable L0 relaxation.
- Strong empirical results: matches or exceeds adapters and full fine-tuning on GLUE and SQuAD with orders-of-magnitude fewer parameters.
- Structured sparsity extension: exploits model grouping to cluster nonzero diffs and further reduce overhead.
- Insightful analysis: per-layer and per-task sparsity patterns reveal where adaptation occurs.

Weaknesses:
- Limited scope: experiments confined to classification and extractive QA; no generative or vision tasks.
- Lack of multi-task/continual evaluation: claims on streaming tasks and amortized storage are not empirically validated.
- Hyperparameter sensitivity: key choices (λ, Hard-Concrete stretch, initial α) lack ablation or robustness study.
- Training overhead: memory/time costs of optimizing mask parameters are discussed only qualitatively.
- Comparison to recent PEFT methods: omits baselines like LoRA or BitFit that also prune or adapt subsets of weights.

Actionable Suggestions:
- Provide ablations on λ, stretch bounds, and mask initialization to assess robustness.
- Quantify training and inference memory and runtime overheads with and without pruning.
- Include experiments in a true multi-task or continual learning setting to validate amortized storage benefits.
- Compare against additional parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods (e.g. LoRA, BitFit) and discuss tradeoffs.
- Discuss limitations and potential extensions (e.g. pretraining for sparse adaptation).

reviews.review:
This paper addresses the important and underexplored problem of out-of-distribution (OOD) detection in multi-label classification. The authors propose SumEnergy, a simple aggregation of per-label energy scores, and provide a thorough theoretical interpretation showing that SumEnergy aligns with joint conditional likelihood and data density. SumEnergy is evaluated on three standard multi-label benchmarks (MS-COCO, PASCAL-VOC, NUS-WIDE) against strong baselines (MaxLogit, MSP, ODIN, Mahalanobis, LOF, Isolation Forest) and yields consistent state-of-the-art improvements, reducing FPR95 by up to 10% without any additional parameters or retraining. A comprehensive ablation study demonstrates that summation fails for other scores (logits, probabilities, Mahalanobis), highlighting the unique compatibility of energy scores with sum aggregation. The paper is well written, experiments are thorough, and results are convincing.

Strengths:
• Novel focus on multi-label OOD detection, filling a gap in prior work.
• Simple, parameter-free method that can be applied to any pretrained multi-label classifier.
• Rigorous theoretical derivation linking SumEnergy to joint likelihood and data density.
• Extensive empirical evaluation on multiple datasets, architectures, and OOD domains.
• Detailed ablations clarifying the importance of energy scoring and aggregation.

Weaknesses:
• Assumes label independence; real-world label dependencies are not addressed.
• OOD test sets are limited to ImageNet subsets and textures; more diverse scenes could strengthen claims.
• Computational cost and threshold selection details are not fully discussed.

Suggestions for Improvement:
1. Evaluate SumEnergy on datasets with known label correlations or graph structures.
2. Include additional OOD domains (e.g., adversarially corrupted or domain-shifted images).
3. Report inference overhead and sensitivity to threshold choice in deployment.
4. Discuss potential extensions to model label dependencies using structured energies.

Overall, SumEnergy is a practically appealing and theoretically grounded contribution that significantly advances OOD detection in multi-label settings.

reviews.review:
This paper studies out-of-distribution (OOD) detection for multi-label classification and introduces SumEnergy, a simple aggregation of label-wise energy scores. The method is theoretically motivated via a joint likelihood interpretation, is parameter-free, and can be applied directly to any pretrained multi-label classifier. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks (MS-COCO, PASCAL-VOC, NUS-WIDE), two architectures (DenseNet, ResNet), and multiple OOD sets (ImageNet subsets, Texture) show that SumEnergy consistently reduces FPR95 by up to 10% over strong baselines (MaxLogit, MSP, ODIN, Mahalanobis) and improves AUROC/AUPR.

Strengths:
• Novel focus: Few prior studies address OOD detection in multi-label settings.
• Theoretical grounding: The joint likelihood derivation clarifies why summation captures both data density and conditional label likelihoods.  
• Simplicity and practicality: Parameter-free test-time scoring makes SumEnergy easy to integrate with existing models.  
• Thorough evaluation: Results across datasets, architectures, OOD types, and ablations demonstrate robustness and clear empirical gains.

Weaknesses:
• Independence assumption: The derivation relies on treating labels as independent, but many multi-label tasks exhibit strong label correlations.  
• Limited OOD scenarios: Only two OOD domains are explored; the method’s behavior under domain shifts (e.g., real-world corruptions, unseen combinations of known labels) is unclear.  
• Computational cost: Summation scales with the number of labels; the paper does not report inference time overhead relative to max-based methods.  
• Missing comparisons: Recent fine-tuning approaches (e.g., auxiliary outlier training) are discussed but not compared.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Evaluate SumEnergy on tasks with structured label dependencies or use a simple dependency model (e.g., label co-occurrence) to test sensitivity.  
2. Include additional OOD tests such as corrupted or adversarial examples, and quantify inference overhead.  
3. Compare with fine-tuning based OOD detectors in the multi-label setting to position SumEnergy in broader practice.  
4. Release code and detailed hyperparameters to facilitate reproducibility and adoption.

Overall, this work provides a clear, compelling advance in multi-label OOD detection and sets a new benchmark while remaining broadly applicable.

• Independence assumption: The derivation relies on treating labels as independent, but many multi-label tasks exhibit strong label correlations.

• Computational cost: Summation scales with the number of labels; the paper does not report inference time overhead relative to max-based methods.  
• Missing comparisons: Recent fine-tuning approaches (e.g., auxiliary outlier training) are discussed but not compared.

reviews.review:
This manuscript addresses the underexplored problem of out-of-distribution detection in multi-label classification by proposing SumEnergy, which aggregates per-label energy scores and interprets the aggregate as a joint likelihood proxy. The paper demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on three common benchmarks (MS-COCO, PASCAL-VOC, NUS-WIDE) and provides a clear theoretical derivation under a label-independence assumption. Strengths:
• Novelty: The extension of energy-based OOD detection to multi-label settings is well motivated and timely.  
• Theoretical grounding: The joint likelihood interpretation is sound under the independence assumption and clarifies why summation outperforms max-based scores.  
• Empirical gains: Consistent and significant FPR95 and AUROC improvements over competitive baselines; zero additional parameters or fine-tuning overhead.  
Weaknesses:
• Independence assumption: Real-world multi-label tasks often exhibit strong label correlations; empirical impact of this misspecification is unexamined.  
• Limited OOD scenarios: Experiments focus on two OOD datasets (ImageNet subset, Textures), leaving open performance on more challenging or domain-specific OOD sources (medical, scene segmentation).  
• Baseline adaptation: Several baselines are originally designed for multi-class; their multimodal adaptation via naive aggregation may not reflect best possible performance.  
• Threshold sensitivity and calibration: The paper omits analysis of threshold selection robustness and calibration of SumEnergy scores across datasets or architectures.  
Actionable suggestions:
1. Evaluate SumEnergy on datasets with explicit label dependencies (e.g., Pascal-Context or structured multi-label benchmarks) and compare against dependency-aware aggregation.  
2. Include additional OOD sources (e.g., synthetic noise, adversarial, medical scans) to assess generality.  
3. Provide ablations on sensitivity to the independence assumption and threshold selection.  
4. Compare against stronger multi-label OOD baselines or fine-tuned methods.  
Overall, the paper makes a solid contribution but requires broader empirical validation and deeper analysis of its core assumptions before acceptance.

reviews.review:
This paper addresses the understudied problem of out-of-distribution (OOD) detection in multi-label classification by proposing SumEnergy, a parameter-free aggregation of per-label energy scores. Strengths:
• Novelty: Extends recent energy-based OOD detection to the multi-label setting and provides a clear joint-likelihood interpretation under label independence.  
• Empirical validation: Thorough experiments on three benchmarks (MS-COCO, PASCAL-VOC, NUS-WIDE) and two architectures (DenseNet, ResNet) demonstrate consistent reductions in FPR95 (up to 10% absolute) and gains in AUROC and AUPR versus strong baselines (MaxLogit, MSP, ODIN, Mahalanobis, LOF, Isolation Forest).  
• Ablations: Systematic study of aggregation (max vs. sum), application of sum to prior scores, and comparison to SumProb illuminate the compatibility of energy scores with summation and justify design choices.
Weaknesses:
• Independence assumption: The theoretical derivation relies on labels being conditionally independent, which often fails in real multi-label data where correlations exist.  
• Limited OOD scenarios: Evaluations use only ImageNet subsets and Textures; performance on more challenging distribution shifts (e.g., medical or scene-specific OOD) is unexplored.  
• Missing comparisons: Does not compare to multi-label variants of fine-tuned OOD detectors (e.g., auxiliary outlier exposure or deep ensembles) that could close the gap.
Actionable suggestions:
1. Evaluate SumEnergy on datasets with known label dependencies and report how correlation degrades performance; consider structured aggregation that models dependencies.  
2. Incorporate OOD fine-tuning baselines (outlier exposure, ensemble methods) to contextualize gains of parameter-free SumEnergy.  
3. Extend experiments to additional OOD domains (e.g., domain shifts in medical imaging or video) to demonstrate broader applicability.  
Overall, the paper makes a well-motivated, solid contribution with clear empirical gains, meriting acceptance.

reviews.review: This paper proposes replacing fully-connected layers with a composition of fixed truncated butterfly transforms and a small dense core, motivated by the Fast Johnson–Lindenstrauss Transform. Strengths: The authors provide a clear theoretical connection (Section 4.2) and a landscape analysis for a linear encoder–decoder network (Section 5, Theorem 1). Empirical results across vision (CIFAR-10, ImageNet), NLP (CoNLL-03, PTB), synthetic and real low-rank data demonstrate substantial parameter reduction, faster convergence, and competitive or superior accuracy compared to standard layers (Section 6.1–6.3). The learned butterfly sketch also outperforms learned sparse sketches in supervised low-rank approximation (Section 7). Weaknesses: The theoretical analysis is restricted to linear encoder–decoder architectures; convergence behavior in deeper nonlinear networks with multiple butterfly replacements is not addressed. The extra logarithmic depth overhead may affect optimization when replacing several layers. Ablation on the choice of k1,k2 and the trade-off between fixed and fully trainable butterfly weights is limited. Comparisons to recent network pruning or tensor-decomposition baselines are missing. Actionable suggestions: Include an ablation study on replacing different numbers of layers and report full-network training stability and final accuracy. Provide detailed latency and memory benchmarks on modern hardware. Extend theory or empirical analysis to nonlinear networks with multiple butterfly blocks. Compare against state-of-the-art pruning and low-rank compression methods. Explore varying k1 and k2 systematically to guide practitioners.

reviews.review:
This paper proposes to replace dense linear layers in neural networks with a three‐stage architecture: a truncated butterfly network (sparse, log-depth) followed by a small dense layer and the transpose of the butterfly. The design is motivated by the Fast Johnson–Lindenstrauss Transform (FJLT) and backed by a novel analysis of the optimization landscape in a linear encoder–decoder setting. Empirically, the authors replace a final fully‐connected layer in vision (EfficientNet, ResNet variants) and NLP (Flair tagger) models, demonstrating a near‐linear reduction in parameters, faster convergence/training time, and matching or superior accuracy/F₁. They further validate in synthetic and real autoencoder tasks, run a two‐phase learning analysis, and extend to supervised sketching in low‐rank matrix approximation, outperforming both random and learned sparse sketches.

Strengths:
- Clear theoretical grounding: connects FJLT approximations to learnable butterfly networks and proves that local minima remain global in the linear encoder–decoder butterﬂy setting.
- Broad empirical evaluation: across vision, NLP, synthetic matrices, and sketching tasks; consistent parameter savings (O(n log n) vs O(n²)) with negligible performance loss.
- Strong baselines: compares to dense layers, random FJLT, sparse sketches, learned sparse sketches, and Gaussian projections.
- Practical impact: reduced memory, faster inference/training, potential energy savings, with code-friendly PyTorch initializations.

Weaknesses:
- Hyperparameter sensitivity: choice k=log n is heuristic; no ablation on performance vs k, depth of butterfly, or tradeoffs in more or fewer parameters.
- Single‐layer replacement: experiments only replace one dense layer; effect of multiple replacements or convolutional replacements is untested.
- Initialization dependence: the role of FJLT‐style initialization vs random is not isolated.
- Reproducibility: detailed hyperparameters, training schedules, random seeds, and open‐sourcing code are not provided.
- Theoretical scope: analysis is limited to linear networks; extension to nonlinear activations or deep stacks is left for future work.

Suggestions:
1) Add ablations varying (k1,k2) and depth; quantify the accuracy/parameter/speed tradeoff. 2) Evaluate replacing multiple dense layers and a convolutional layer to demonstrate generality. 3) Include controlled experiments on initialization (FJLT vs random) to show its effect on training convergence. 4) Release code, full hyperparameter settings, and random seeds to support reproducibility. 5) Discuss practical overhead of butterfly graph operations (e.g., sparse kernels) and their integration into deep learning frameworks.

Overall, this paper makes a compelling case for sparse, structured linear transforms by combining theory and practice. It merits acceptance at ICLR.

reviews.review: This paper proposes to replace dense linear layers in neural networks with a fixed sparse “butterfly” structure motivated by fast Johnson–Lindenstrauss transforms. The authors develop (i) a theoretical analysis of the encoder–decoder landscape when the encoder uses a truncated butterfly network, and (ii) extensive empirical validation on vision and NLP benchmarks plus a supervised sketching task. Strengths: 1. The idea of parameterizing linear layers via a fixed sparse fast-transform graph is well grounded in FJLT literature. 2. Theoretical results (Theorem 1) rigorously characterize critical points and worst‐case guarantees in a two‐phase training of linear encoder–decoders. 3. Broad empirical evaluation: replacing FC layers in ResNets, EfficientNet, and sequence taggers, showing comparable or slightly improved accuracy, faster convergence, and parameter reductions. 4. Novel supervised sketching experiments demonstrating learned butterfly sketches outperform learned sparse Johnson–Lindenstrauss matrices. Weaknesses: 1. Novelty is incremental: many prior works (e.g. Moczulski et al. 2016, Alizadeh et al. 2020) have explored FFT‐like structured layers; the main theoretical landscape result applies only to linear autoencoders, not full nonlinear stacks. 2. Ablation studies are limited: sensitivity to choice of k, impact of additional depth on gradient flow, and comparisons to other compression methods (low‐rank, circulant) are missing. 3. Overhead from increased depth is not fully quantified: wall‐clock trade‐offs depend on implementation details and hardware. 4. Theoretical assumptions (distinct eigenvalues, invertibility of BXXᵀBᵀ) are strong and their practical validity is not discussed. Suggestions: include ablations on non‐logarithmic k choices, measure gradient norms and training stability with varying depth, compare against SVD/truncated SVD and other structured transforms, and discuss limitations of theoretical assumptions in practice.

reviews.review:
This manuscript proposes replacing standard dense linear layers in neural networks with a composition of truncated butterfly networks and a small dense core, leveraging Fast Johnson–Lindenstrauss Transform (FJLT) theory. Strengths:
• The idea is grounded in well-established FJLT results, yielding a nearly linear parameter count instead of quadratic, with a clear theoretical justification (Proposition 1) for preservation of the layer’s action.  
• Empirical studies on vision (EfficientNet, ResNet variants, ImageNet/CIFAR) and NLP (named-entity and POS tagging) demonstrate matching or superior accuracy, faster convergence, and reduced training/inference time.  
• Theoretical analysis (Theorem 1) of the encoder–decoder network with a fixed butterfly encoder offers insight into the optimization landscape, showing no loss in representational power for auto-encoders and enabling worst-case guarantees via two-phase training.  
• A supervised sketching experiment extends recent work on learned sparse pre-processing (Indyk et al.), showing that a learned butterfly sketch outperforms both random and learned sparse matrices in low-rank approximation tasks.  
Weaknesses:
• The theoretical guarantees are restricted to linear encoder–decoder settings; convergence and critical-point analysis for general deep nonlinear networks with inserted butterfly layers remain unaddressed.  
• Ablation studies on the choice of intermediate ranks (k1,k2), the effect of butterfly depth on optimization, and replacing multiple dense layers are limited.  
• Comparisons to a broader set of recent structured-transform baselines (e.g., low-displacement-rank, Toeplitz-like layers) are brief.  
Actionable suggestions:
1) Include ablation experiments replacing multiple dense layers and varying k1,k2 to study depth vs. performance and parameter-efficiency trade-offs.  
2) Extend (or empirically probe) optimization behavior in networks with activations/nonlinearities and multiple inserted butterfly modules.  
3) Compare more extensively against other structured transforms and provide hardware-level runtime benchmarks.  
4) Release code to facilitate reproducibility and adoption.

reviews.review: This paper explores emergent gesture-based communication in a continuous, high-dimensional multi-agent setting, proposing that energy cost and a Zipf-distributed intent prior induce a latent structure enabling zero-shot (ZS) coordination. Strengths: 1) Novel domain: extends emergent communication to embodied agents actuating joint trajectories in 3D, a realistic modality for robotics and human–machine interaction. 2) Clear formalization: the combination of energy regularization and nonuniform intent frequencies is well motivated, with analytic discussion of optimization challenges in continuous action spaces. 3) Thorough empirical analysis: ablations on Zipf vs uniform, implicit vs explicit structure, curricula, and discrete analogues clarify when ZS generalization is possible. 4) Actionable insights: demonstrating that providing energy values to a third-party observer yields substantially higher ZS accuracy highlights the separate roles of protocol induction vs discovery. Weaknesses: 1) Limited scale: experiments use only up to 10 intents and a simple four-joint robot arm; results may not transfer to richer morphologies or larger vocabularies. 2) Moderate ZS performance: best cross-play accuracy (≈56% for 10 intents) remains only modestly above the most-frequent-class baseline (34%), suggesting brittle generalization. 3) Missing comparisons: no evaluation against alternative grounding signals (e.g. distance traveled, entropy penalties) or against recent discrete ZS coordination methods. 4) Underexplored real-world robustness: the simulator omits obstacles, dynamics variability, or human data. Actionable suggestions: • Scale to more complex agents (e.g. bipedal morphologies) and more intents to assess generality. • Compare energy grounding against other continuous-cost priors or message quantization strategies. • Incorporate domain noise (varying link masses, joint limits) to test robustness. • Evaluate on real robotic hardware or a photorealistic simulator to demonstrate practical applicability. Overall, the work opens a promising direction but would benefit from broader empirical validation.

reviews.review:
This manuscript introduces an intriguing extension of emergent communication to embodied agents, where continuous multi‐step motion trajectories serve as a communicative channel. The authors identify energy cost and nonuniform intent distributions (Zipf) as common‐knowledge inductive biases that can ground gesture protocols for zero‐shot (ZS) coordination. They further analyze training challenges in high‐dimensional continuous action spaces and propose two interventions—providing explicit energy values and a torque‐based curriculum—to improve ZS performance.

Strengths: The paper tackles an underexplored modality (articulated motion) and convincingly motivates energy as a universal latent variable. The theoretical framing of implicit versus explicit latent structure is clear, and the continuous‐action referential game is well specified. Ablations across intent set sizes (2, 5, 10) and the inclusion of a discrete toy domain provide useful insight into the combinatorial ordering problem. The analysis of energy distributions and training dynamics is thoughtful, and visualizations of learned gestures aid interpretability.

Weaknesses: The evaluation remains limited to a single simulated morphology and small intent sets, and ZS accuracies plateau below practical thresholds for larger vocabularies. Baseline comparisons to other ZS methods (e.g. other‐play [Hu et al., ICML’20]) are absent. Hyperparameter sensitivity, variance across runs, and statistical significance are not fully reported. The torque curriculum shows mixed benefits, and its design choices need deeper justification. The manuscript would benefit from clearer details on reproducibility (learning rates, compute costs) and discussion of real‐world constraints (sensor noise, body morphology diversity).

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Compare against existing ZS coordination baselines (other‐play, symmetric initialization) in both discrete and continuous settings.
2. Report mean±std over multiple random seeds and include statistical tests.
3. Extend experiments to additional morphologies or noise levels to assess generality.
4. Provide full hyperparameter tables and code to facilitate replication.
5. Discuss how gesture semantics could be adapted when energy cost is not the only grounding cue.

reviews.review:
This work explores emergent zero-shot communication in embodied agents by having them gesture via continuous joint control in a simulated 3D environment. Under a non-uniform Zipf distribution over intents and a common energy cost term, the authors show that agents can induce a latent monotonic relationship between energy exertion and concept frequency. They evaluate both discrete toy domains and a continuous-action referential game with 2–10 intents, using a third-party observer to measure generalization to novel partners. Key interventions include providing explicit energy values to the observer and employing a torque-minimization curriculum.

Strengths:
• Novel modality: First study of emergent communication through embodied joint trajectories rather than symbolic channels.

reviews.review: This paper investigates emergent zero-shot communication in embodied multi-agent populations, where agents signal intents via joint trajectories rather than discrete messages. The core contribution is combining an energy cost penalty with a nonuniform (Zipf) intent distribution to induce a monotonic mapping between intent frequency and motion energy, and evaluating generalization to novel partners via an external observer. Strengths: 1) Novel modality: extending emergent communication to continuous, physically grounded gestures is an important step toward realistic embodied AI. 2) Clear identification of challenges: the authors characterize the combinatorial ordering and local‐optima issues in continuous action spaces. 3) Empirical ablations: Zipf only vs. Zipf+energy, and curriculum vs. no curriculum, across tasks with 2, 5, and 10 intents. Weaknesses: 1) Limited scope: experiments use a simple 4‐joint arm morphology in a toy referential game. It is unclear how results scale to richer bodies or tasks involving goal‐directed behavior beyond intent classification. 2) Baseline comparison: existing zero‐shot coordination methods (e.g., Other‐Play) are not evaluated in the embodied setting, making it hard to judge relative improvement. 3) Evaluation metrics: reporting only accuracy ignores analysis of gesture interpretability or robustness to noise variations. 4) Low ZS performance on larger intent sets (peak 56%) suggests brittle generalization. Actionable suggestions: • Include comparisons with established ZS coordination algorithms adapted to continuous channels. • Scale to more complex morphologies or mixed‐purpose tasks (manipulation+communication). • Evaluate the impact of different energy regularizers or alternative common‐knowledge priors. • Provide qualitative user studies or interpretability analyses of learned gestures. Overall, the paper opens a promising direction but requires stronger baselines and richer evaluations to fully demonstrate impact.

reviews.review:
This paper addresses the challenge of learning invariant explanations in deep learning by formalizing the principle that “good explanations are hard to vary.” The authors introduce a consistency score for minima—Invariant Learning Consistency (ILC)—and show that standard gradient averaging (a logical OR of environment‐specific gradients) can favor spurious “patchwork” solutions. They propose an alternative aggregation (“logical AND”) operationalized via an AND‐mask on per‐example gradients. Theoretical analyses connect consistency to the geometric mean of Hessians and demonstrate that the AND‐mask suppresses random or inconsistent signals exponentially fast. Empirically, the method recovers a true invariant “spiral” mechanism in a synthetic dataset where domain‐agnostic baselines fail, prevents memorization of CIFAR‐10 with random labels, resists label noise, and improves generalization in behavioral cloning on CoinRun.

Strengths:
• Clear formalism: The ILC metric and connection to arithmetic vs. geometric gradient averaging are novel and well‐motivated.  
• The AND‐mask is lightweight and efficient, with theoretical convergence guarantees.  
• Comprehensive experiments demonstrate efficacy on synthetic, vision, and RL‐based tasks.

Weaknesses:
• The AND‐mask introduces a new hyperparameter (agreement threshold) whose sensitivity is not fully characterized.  
• The synthetic spiral task is illustrative but limited; more real‐world OOD benchmarks would strengthen claims.  
• Handling sign conflicts and partial agreement in nonconvex settings requires further elaboration.

Suggestions:
1. Include an ablation on threshold sensitivity and guidelines for choosing τ.  
2. Evaluate on established OOD benchmarks (e.g., Colored MNIST, PACS) to benchmark against IRM and domain‐adaptation methods.  
3. Explore adaptive or learned aggregation strategies beyond hard masking to address sign conflicts.  

Overall, this work offers a compelling new perspective on gradient aggregation for invariance and merits acceptance.

reviews.review:
This paper introduces a formal measure, Invariant Learning Consistency (ILC), to quantify the desideratum “good explanations are hard to vary” in deep learning. The authors define a consistency score for minima of the pooled loss across multiple environments, link it to the geometric vs. arithmetic mean of Hessians, and propose a practical AND-mask that zeros gradient components whose signs disagree across environments. Theoretical analysis (under diagonal Hessian and Gaussian gradient assumptions) shows that the AND-mask fosters convergence to invariant directions and suppresses random or spurious signals at an exponential rate. Empirical evaluation includes
– A challenging synthetic memorization dataset where spirals (the invariant mechanism) must be learned despite easy linear “shortcuts,”
– CIFAR-10 random-label memorization and label-noise experiments showing the AND-mask prevents overfitting,
– A behavioral cloning task on CoinRun demonstrating improved generalization to unseen levels.

Strengths:
• A novel, well-motivated formalization of consistency grounded in causal invariance and Deutsch’s “hard to vary” principle.
• A simple, computationally efficient AND-mask algorithm with theoretical guarantees.
• Clear synthetic and real-task experiments demonstrating prevention of memorization and improved o.o.d. generalization.
• Code release and extensive appendix detail.

Weaknesses:
• Evaluation is limited to one synthetic benchmark and two tasks; broader domain-generalization benchmarks (e.g. PACS, VLCS) are missing.
• Comparison is restricted to IRM and DANN; recent methods such as REx or Fishr are not considered.
• Theoretical analysis relies on diagonal Hessians and convex approximations that may not hold in deep non-convex models.
• The AND-mask introduces a hyperparameter (agreement threshold) whose sensitivity and selection in practice needs clarification.

Actionable suggestions:
1. Evaluate on standard domain-generalization benchmarks (PACS, VLCS) and compare against Group DRO, REx, Fishr.
2. Provide an ablation of threshold sensitivity, its impact on convergence speed and generalization.
3. Discuss limitations when environments are not clearly defined or highly imbalanced.
4. Analyze runtime overhead on large CNNs and strategies to integrate AND-mask in existing frameworks.

Overall, the paper offers a compelling new perspective on aggregating learning signals to enforce invariance and presents strong empirical evidence. I recommend acceptance.

reviews.review: This paper introduces Invariant Learning Consistency (ILC) as a formal measure of how “hard to vary” an explanation is across environments, and proposes a practical AND-mask algorithm that suppresses gradient components with inconsistent signs. The authors provide a clear theoretical motivation, link ILC to arithmetic vs. geometric Hessian means, and validate on (i) a synthetic spirals+shortcuts dataset, (ii) CIFAR-10 memorization and label-noise tasks, and (iii) a behavioral cloning task in CoinRun. Strengths: 1. The ILC formalism rigorously captures an intuitive invariance notion. 2. The AND-mask is conceptually simple and shows strong performance on the synthetic benchmark. 3. Experiments convincingly prevent memorization on random labels without harming standard generalization. 4. The paper offers both theoretical bounds (e.g. for random gradients) and empirical analysis of gradient correlations. Weaknesses: 1. The synthetic dataset, while illustrative, is low-dimensional; it is unclear how the AND-mask scales to high-dimensional natural data or large batch sizes. 2. The choice of mask threshold τ is heuristic; there is no ablation on sensitivity or guidelines for tuning. 3. The computational overhead of per-example gradient signs is not quantified, and current DL frameworks may be inefficient. 4. The CoinRun results show modest KL improvements and lack policy-level evaluation. 5. Comparison to other invariant learning methods (beyond IRM) is limited. Actionable suggestions: • Provide an ablation study on τ and batch size, and guidelines for selecting τ in practice. • Report wall-clock overhead and memory cost of the AND-mask versus standard training. • Evaluate on at least one larger-scale vision or RL generalization benchmark (e.g. PACS or ProcGen RL). • Include additional comparisons to gradient-agreement or robustness baselines. • Clarify implementation details (random seeds, hyperparameter ranges) to enhance reproducibility.

reviews.review: This paper formalizes the principle “good explanations are hard to vary” by introducing a consistency score for minima across environments and proposes a practical AND-mask that suppresses gradient components with inconsistent signs. Strengths: (1) The Invariant Learning Consistency (ILC) framework provides a clear theoretical lens linking consistency to arithmetic vs. geometric means of Hessians. (2) The AND-mask is simple, cheap to implement, and shown to prevent shortcut learning on a synthetic dataset, random‐label memorization on CIFAR-10, label‐noise overfitting, and a CoinRun behavioral-cloning task. (3) The synthetic dataset with controlled invariant vs. spurious features offers a compelling dissection of learning signals. Weaknesses: (1) The theoretical results are confined to convex/quadratic approximations; nonconvex convergence guarantees are lacking. (2) The AND-mask introduces a new hyperparameter (threshold) whose sensitivity and interaction with optimizers (Adam, batch size) require more systematic study. (3) Empirical evaluation is limited to synthetic, small‐scale vision, and CoinRun; standard real‐world OOD benchmarks (e.g. WILDS, DomainBed) are missing. (4) Comparison to IRM and other invariance methods could probe optimization costs and stability. Actionable suggestions: (a) Perform ablations over threshold, batch size, and learning rate to characterize robustness. (b) Evaluate on established domain generalization datasets to demonstrate broader applicability. (c) Analyze computational overhead and convergence behavior in larger-scale, nonconvex models. (d) Extend theoretical analysis toward nonconvex settings or smaller toy networks to elucidate when AND-mask succeeds or fails.

reviews.review: This paper addresses the important problem of combining provable adversarial robustness with detection by introducing an abstain class into a certifiably robust classifier. The authors adapt Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) to jointly train a verifier for both classification and detection, derive a dual formulation for efficient bound computation, and demonstrate empirical benefits on MNIST and CIFAR-10. Strengths: 1. Novel integration of detection into certified defenses. 2. Clear theoretical derivations, including convex relaxations and dual certificates. 3. Modest computational overhead over standard IBP training. 4. Empirical gains in provable robust error on CIFAR-10 for larger perturbations. Weaknesses: 1. Absolute robust error rates remain high (>60% error at ε=8/255 on CIFAR-10). 2. Natural accuracy drops substantially, and abstaining on clean inputs may be problematic in practice. 3. Evaluation is limited to small datasets and a single architecture; generalization to larger vision tasks is unclear. 4. Adaptive attack evaluation uses PGD only; stronger or black-box attacks are not considered. 5. Hyperparameter choices for η, κ, and λ are heuristic and lack ablation. Suggestions: • Report ROC curves showing detection vs. misclassification trade-off on clean and adversarial inputs. • Include results on additional benchmarks (e.g., SVHN or Tiny ImageNet) and larger architectures to assess scalability. • Perform ablation studies on η and κ schedules and analyze sensitivity. • Evaluate against stronger adaptive attacks targeting both classification and detection. • Discuss practical deployment considerations when abstaining on legitimate inputs. Overall, this work makes a meaningful step in certifiable detection but would benefit from broader empirical validation and ablation.

reviews.review:
This paper presents a novel extension of interval bound propagation (IBP) defenses by augmenting a standard K-class classifier with an additional abstain/detection class, yielding the first joint training and certification of robust classification plus detection. The authors derive convex relaxations (Theorems 1–3) to certify that perturbed inputs are either correctly classified or abstained, and propose an efficient analytic algorithm (Alg. 1) to compute dual bounds. A combined training loss balances natural accuracy, standard robust loss, and abstain-aware robust loss. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 show improved certified error rates—especially at larger perturbations—compared to IBP alone, and detailed Pareto‐front analyses demonstrate effective trade-offs between natural error and robustness by leveraging detection.

Strengths:
• First provable certification framework incorporating a detection/abstain option. 
• Rigorous theoretical formulation with convex dual derivations and an O(n log n) algorithm. 
• Empirical validation showing reduced verified error, and clear Pareto frontier comparisons.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Evaluation scope is limited to small datasets and modest architectures. Extend to larger-scale models or more complex tasks (e.g. SVHN, TinyImageNet) to demonstrate generality.
2. Hyperparameter sensitivity (λ₁, λ₂, η schedules) and abstention rate control are not fully explored. Provide ablation studies and guidance on tuning, and quantify the fraction of natural images abstained.
3. Compare against other detection methods (e.g. Laidlaw & Feizi 2019) under adaptive attacks, and report ROC curves or cost‐sensitive metrics to assess practical utility.
4. Discuss deployment trade-offs: how to set an abstention budget and handle user feedback when abstains occur.
5. Improve clarity by summarizing main algorithm steps in the body, and release code for reproducibility.

Overall, the paper makes a clear theoretical and empirical contribution toward certified detection, but would benefit from broader evaluation and more extensive ablations to fully assess applicability and robustness.

reviews.review:
This paper introduces a novel framework for joint provable robustness and adversarial detection by augmenting a K-way classifier with an abstain/detection class and extending Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) to certify both classification and detection guarantees. The authors derive a dual formulation for the new specification, provide an analytic solver for the last layer bound, and integrate the resulting certificate into training with minimal overhead. Empirical results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 show improvements over standard IBP, particularly at larger ε values.

Strengths:
• Novelty: First certifiable detector integrated with robust certification methods. Extending IBP to detection is conceptually clean and computationally efficient.  
• Theoretical contribution: Convex relaxation proofs and analytic solver for the abstain-bound increase transparency and reproducibility.  
• Strong empirical gains: Demonstrates consistent reduction in verified error and adaptive PGD success rate.  
• Training stability: Careful scheduler design and parameter ramping are well documented.

Weaknesses:
• Limited evaluation: Only small datasets (MNIST, CIFAR-10) and IBP baseline; omits comparisons to tighter relaxations (CROWN, deep poly) or larger-scale tasks.  
• Detection trade-off: Impact on natural accuracy and false-abstain rates are reported but lack ROC-style analysis or precision/recall metrics.  
• Hyperparameter sensitivity: Choice of η, α and scheduling appears empirical; more ablations are needed.  

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Evaluate on additional benchmarks (e.g. SVHN) and against other verifiable defenses (CROWN, SDP).  
2. Quantify detection performance via ROC curves, false-positive/negative rates, and application-dependent metrics.  
3. Include ablation studies on η and α scheduling, and discuss tightness of the new bound relative to standard IBP.  

Overall, this is a solid contribution in verifiable adversarial defenses with promising directions for extension.

reviews.review:
This paper introduces a method for jointly training a provably robust classifier and detector by augmenting a K-class network with an (K+1)th “abstain” label and extending Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) to certify both classification and detection. The core idea is to reformulate the certification and training objectives so that adversarial examples are either correctly classified or assigned to the abstain class, and to upper- and lower-bound the corresponding loss via convex relaxations of the final layer and dual variables. Empirical results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 show modest improvements in verified robust error, especially at larger perturbation budgets, and the paper provides theoretical guarantees via Theorems 1–3 and an efficient algorithm for computing the certification bounds.

Strengths:
- The extension of IBP to include detection is conceptually simple, well motivated, and supported by clear derivations and proofs.
- Algorithm 1 for efficient counterexample bounds and the dual formulation are well described and implementable.
- Empirical results demonstrate an ability to trade natural accuracy for certified robust accuracy by abstaining on high-uncertainty inputs, yielding lower verified errors than vanilla IBP at significant perturbation radii.

Weaknesses:
- The abstain-class approach is incremental and relies on IBP; it does not leverage tighter relaxations such as IBP-CROWN or randomized smoothing for stronger guarantees.
- Experimental evaluation omits comparisons to state-of-the-art verifiable defenses beyond IBP (e.g., LP-based or semi-definite methods) and does not assess false positive rates or detection precision on natural data.
- Ablation on hyperparameters (λ1, λ2, η ramp) and sensitivity to the abstain threshold is lacking.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Include experiments incorporating tighter relaxations (IBP-CROWN) or compare against recent mixed-integer or semi-definite verifiers.
2. Report detailed detection metrics (abstain rate, false alarms) on held-out natural samples.
3. Provide ablations on the trade-off parameters (λ1, λ2, η) and discuss practical deployment criteria (when to abstain).
4. Evaluate on a larger-scale benchmark (e.g., TinyImageNet) to establish scalability.

Overall, the paper offers a neat proof-of-concept for certifiable detection, but requires broader empirical validation and a more thorough comparison to stronger baselines.

reviews.review:
Summary: This paper tackles the problem of decentralized attribution for generative models. Instead of a centralized multiclass classifier, the authors propose user-specific binary linear classifiers (keys) that distinguish model outputs from authentic data. They derive sufficient geometric conditions on the keys—data compliance and mutual orthogonality—that guarantee a lower bound on attribution accuracy. They validate the theory on MNIST, CelebA and FFHQ using DCGAN, StyleGAN and test robustness under adversarial post-processing.

Strengths:
• Rigorous theory: The paper presents clear propositions and theorems linking attribution performance to key geometry, data distribution and perturbation magnitude.  
• Simple design rules: Data-compliant (negative on authentic data) and near-orthogonal keys emerge naturally and are easy to implement.  
• Extensive experiments: Empirical support on three datasets, two architectures, and five post-processing attacks. Trade-off between FID and robustness is carefully quantified.  
• Novel decentralized protocol: Shifts from monolithic central classifier to scalable per-user watermarks with attribution guarantees.

Weaknesses:
• Linear classifiers only: The current scheme relies on linear decision boundaries; nonlinear extensions are left for future work.  
• Key management at scale: As the number of users grows, computing and storing orthogonal keys becomes a sphere-packing challenge.  
• Generation quality loss: Perturbations and robust training notably degrade FID scores, especially on high-resolution data.

Suggestions:
1. Extend theoretical analysis to nonlinear attribution models (e.g., kernel or small neural nets) and derive sufficient conditions.  
2. Propose approximate or hierarchical key-generation schemes to address sphere-packing and capacity limitations.  
3. Conduct user studies or perceptual metrics to better evaluate the visual impact of perturbations.  
4. Include ablations on δ and γ parameters to clarify sensitivity and guide practitioners.  
5. Investigate adaptive robustness training methods with theoretical backing to reduce quality loss.

reviews.review:
This paper presents a decentralized approach for attributing outputs of generative models by assigning each user a binary linear classifier parameterized by a unit-norm key vector. The authors derive sufficient conditions for (1) distinguishability of each watermarked generator from authentic data and (2) collective attributability across N users, linking these guarantees to data geometry, key–key angles, and retraining perturbations. They validate the theory on MNIST, CelebA, and FFHQ with DCGAN and StyleGAN, and explore robustness–quality trade-offs under common post-processing attacks.

Strengths:
• Novel decentralized attribution framework that scales beyond centralized multi-class classifiers.  
• Rigorous theoretical analysis yielding intuitive design rules: data compliance (φᵀx<0) and near-orthogonality of keys.  
• Comprehensive experiments: theorem validation, key generation ablation, FID quality measures, and robustness against blur, crop, noise, JPEG, and combined attacks.  
• Clear motivation for model forensics and copyright protection.

Weaknesses:
• Linearity assumption (f(x)=sign(φᵀx)) may limit applicability to high-dimensional, nonlinear feature spaces; extensions to kernel or deep classifiers are not studied.  
• Key capacity remains an open sphere-packing problem; no practical bound or heuristic for large user populations.  
• Requiring retraining of each user model may impose significant overhead; security against adaptive removal or collusion attacks is not analyzed.  
• Lack of comparison to alternative decentralized watermarking/fingerprinting baselines (e.g., encoder–decoder networks [Skripniuk et al., 2020]).

Suggestions:
1. Investigate nonlinear or learned embeddings for keys to relax the linearity constraint.  
2. Empirically estimate maximum feasible key sets and propose efficient key-generation heuristics.  
3. Benchmark against existing decentralized watermarks to quantify practical gains.  
4. Provide security analysis under adaptive adversaries or key inversion attempts.

Overall, the work makes a meaningful theoretical and empirical contribution to model attribution and merits acceptance.

reviews.review:
This paper addresses the scalability and provable guarantees of generative model attribution by introducing a decentralized protocol in which each user-end model is associated with a binary linear classifier parameterized by a user-specific key. Theoretical contributions include sufficient conditions (Theorems 1–2) relating attributability and distinguishability to key geometry, data compliance, and perturbation magnitude. The paper also proposes efficient algorithms for key generation and for retraining user-end models, and validates the approach on MNIST, CelebA, and FFHQ, including robustness experiments under blurring, cropping, noise, JPEG, and combined attacks.

Strengths:
- Novel decentralized attribution framework that scales with the number of models.
- Rigorous derivation of sufficient conditions connecting key orthogonality and data geometry to attribution guarantees.
- Practical algorithmic procedures and empirical validation on diverse datasets and architectures (DCGAN, StyleGAN, PGAN, CycleGAN).
- Insightful discussion of key capacity as a sphere-packing problem and subspace selection via Jacobian eigenanalysis.

Weaknesses:
- The analysis is restricted to linear classifiers; extensions to nonlinear discriminators are not developed.
- Estimating Σ and µ for Theorems 1–2 is computationally costly in high dimensions and only demonstrated on MNIST/CelebA.
- Robust training (Eq. 12) cannot be characterized by the theoretical framework, and combination attacks still break attributability.
- Experiments on FFHQ lack statistical validation of σ, limiting the strength of theoretical claims in high-resolution settings.

Actionable suggestions:
1. Extend or empirically evaluate the framework with nonlinear classifiers (e.g., shallow neural nets) and derive corresponding conditions.
2. Investigate scalable approximations for Σ estimation, such as random projections or low-rank assumptions.
3. Strengthen robustness analysis by integrating theoretic guarantees for adversarial post-processing or by exploring stronger attack models.
4. Provide capacity bounds or heuristics for maximal key sets under generation-quality constraints, beyond the sphere-packing discussion.
5. Include user studies or quantitative metrics on perceptual quality trade-offs when selecting keys from different eigenspaces.

reviews.review: This paper addresses the important problem of attributing content generated by untrusted, user‐end generative models without relying on a centralized multi‐class classifier. The authors propose a decentralized scheme in which each user model is associated with a binary linear classifier parameterized by a user‐specific key. They derive sufficient conditions—based on data compliance, inter‐key orthogonality, and statistical error bounds—for individual model distinguishability and collective attributability. Empirical validation on MNIST, CelebA, and FFHQ with DCGAN and StyleGAN architectures supports the theoretical claims, and the authors explore robustness against common post‐processing attacks (blurring, cropping, noise, JPEG). Strengths: 1) Well‐motivated by real threats like deepfakes and copyright violation. 2) Novel decentralized framework that avoids retraining a large multiclass discriminator. 3) Rigorous theoretical analysis linking geometry of keys with performance guarantees. 4) Extensive experiments across datasets and architectures, including robustness studies. Weaknesses: 1) Reliance on linear classifiers and Gaussian error assumptions limits applicability to more complex models or adversarial settings. 2) Scalability and computational overhead of key generation and per‐model retraining are not fully characterized. 3) Sphere‐packing capacity of the key space remains an open challenge. 4) No evaluation against adaptive, learned attacks aimed at removing or obfuscating keys. Actionable suggestions: • Extend the framework to nonlinear classifiers or embed keys via deep networks and validate theoretical conditions. • Benchmark against learned adversarial removal techniques to assess security under stronger attacks. • Characterize computation time and memory requirements for large user populations. • Develop heuristics for approximate sphere packing to estimate maximum user capacity. • Discuss deployment considerations when the registry has incomplete knowledge of post‐processing functions.

reviews.review:
This paper investigates whether common road rules (e.g., lane discipline, traffic‐light compliance, right‐of‐way conventions) can emerge from multi‐agent reinforcement learning (MARL) when agents optimize for traffic flow under noisy perception. By training agents with sparse LiDAR, destination rewards, and collision penalties in 2D intersections, highways, and nuScenes‐derived maps, the authors demonstrate the spontaneous emergence of seven social driving behaviors. They systematically analyze the impact of perception noise and agent density, and extend to emergent communication via turn‐signal channels. Strengths:
• Empirical breadth: The manuscript covers seven distinct behaviors (traffic lights, lane following, right‐of‐way, fast lanes, safety gap, crosswalk yielding, communication), providing both qualitative rollouts and quantitative metrics.
• Insightful ablations: Varying LiDAR noise and population density convincingly shows these factors are critical ingredients for rule emergence.
• Practical open‐source contribution: The released 2D benchmark can stimulate further MARL driving research.
Weaknesses:
• Simplified simulation: 2D top‐down with idealized LiDAR and limited action space may not translate to realistic 3D driving.
• Limited comparison: The paper lacks baselines (e.g., imitation learning, rule‐based controllers) to contextualize performance.
• Convergence and scalability: Training is slow on larger maps; lane‐change behaviors are precluded by spline constraints.
Actionable suggestions:
1. Include baseline comparisons (e.g., rule‐based or behavior cloning) on the same tasks to quantify gains.
2. Evaluate generalization to novel map geometries or mixed human agents.
3. Extend the action space to allow lane changes and richer maneuvers, and report convergence rates.

reviews.review:
This paper studies how social driving behaviors (“road rules” like traffic-light obedience, lane following, right-of-way, emergent communication, fast lanes, safety distance, crosswalk yielding) can arise in a multi-agent RL environment without explicit rule encoding. Agents equipped with noisy 2D LiDAR and rewarded to reach destinations without collisions learn qualitatively human-like conventions. The authors systematically ablate perception noise and agent density, showing these are key factors for rule emergence, and release an open-source 2D driving suite.

Strengths:
• Novel framing: demonstrating that simple MDP design choices (noise, density) suffice to induce diverse traffic conventions, rather than hand-coding rules.
• Comprehensive empirical evaluation: seven distinct behaviors with both qualitative visualizations and quantitative metrics.
• Insightful analysis: clear demonstration that perception noise and spatial density drive emergence of social rules.
• Open-source release: facilitating replication and future MARL work in driving.

Weaknesses:
• Simplified 2D environments and synthetic LiDAR limit realism; no experiments in 3D or with vision-based sensors.
• No comparison to strong baselines (e.g., rule-based controllers, imitation-learned policies) to quantify benefits of emergent rules.
• Reward-function ablation is missing: unclear which reward components most influence each behavior.
• Scalability to larger, more complex maps or real-world sensor noise remains untested.
• Reproducibility details (hyperparameters, compute resources, training curves) are under-specified.
• Some recent MARL driving works (e.g., Zhou et al. 2020 SMARTS) are not empirically compared.

Actionable suggestions:
1. Include ablations removing or varying reward terms to assess their impact on emergent behaviors.
2. Benchmark against rule-based and imitation-learning baselines to demonstrate gains in safety or efficiency.
3. Test generalization on unseen, larger, or more realistic map layouts and sensor models (e.g., camera noise).
4. Provide full training details: hyperparameters, hardware, runtimes, and learning curves.
5. Incorporate or compare to recent multi-agent driving platforms (SMARTS).

Overall, the study offers valuable insights into environment design for social behavior emergence but requires stronger baselines and realism to fully validate its significance.

reviews.review:
Summary:
This paper investigates the emergence of human-like road rules in decentralized multi-agent driving environments trained via PPO. By introducing perception noise and varying agent density, the authors demonstrate that behaviors such as obeying traffic lights, lane following, right-of-way, safe following distance, emergent turn signals, and “fast lanes” arise as optimal strategies for maximizing throughput without explicit rule encoding.

Strengths:
1. Novelty: Proposes a lightweight 2D MARL framework to study socially accepted driving conventions.
2. Comprehensive empirical analysis: Ablations on LiDAR noise, agent density, and communication clearly link environment design choices to emergent behaviors.
3. Broad set of emergent behaviors: Seven qualitatively distinct traffic rules are demonstrated and quantified.
4. Open resources: Code and documentation are released to facilitate reproducibility.

Weaknesses:
1. Limited realism: The 2D kinematic model, uniform LiDAR-drop noise, and discretized action sets omit key vehicle dynamics and sensor distortions, limiting generalization to real-world settings.
2. Reward hyperparameters underspecified: No sensitivity analysis on regularization coefficients or reward component weights; reproducibility suffers from missing network architecture and seed details.
3. No strong baselines: Lacks comparison to imitation learning or hard-coded rule-based policies to quantify throughput or safety improvements.
4. Action-space constraints prevent lane-change emergence; the spline parameterization predefines paths, contradicting claims of spontaneous lane negotiation.
5. Presentation issues: Inconsistent citation formatting, several typos in equations (stray unicode codes), low-resolution figures without axis units.

Suggestions:
– Provide detailed hyperparameter tables and seeds; include sensitivity studies on reward terms.
– Compare against simple rule-based/autonomous baselines on key metrics.
– Extend to more realistic dynamics or richer perception models.
– Refine notation and polish figures for clarity.

Overall, despite valuable insights into emergent traffic conventions, the methodological and presentation gaps currently outweigh the contributions.

reviews.review:
This paper investigates whether common road rules — such as obeying traffic lights, maintaining lanes, and yielding right of way — can emerge from training multiple autonomous agents in a shared 2D driving environment using only a generic goal-reaching reward and noisy LiDAR observations. The authors design ablations over perception noise, agent density, and action parameterizations (fixed track vs. spline) to show that perception uncertainty and spatial congestion are critical for emergent behavior. They release code and synthetic maps to support reproducibility.

Strengths:
• The focus on environment design (reward, noise, density) rather than hand-coded rules offers a fresh perspective on scalable simulation for social driving behaviors.  
• Comprehensive empirical study: seven distinct behaviors (stop at lights, lane following, right-of-way, communication, fast-lane formation, safety distance, pedestrian avoidance) are both qualitatively visualized and quantitatively measured.  
• Ablation experiments convincingly isolate the roles of noise and density, providing actionable insights for MARL simulator construction.

Weaknesses:
• The 2D physics and simplified LiDAR model limit realism; it is unclear if these findings transfer to high-fidelity 3D simulators with realistic sensors and dynamics.  
• No direct comparison to imitation learning or rule-based baselines (e.g., supervised driving policies) to quantify the benefit of emergent rules.  
• The bilevel spline/acceleration formulation prevents lane-change emergence and may overconstrain agent behavior.

Suggestions:
1. Evaluate the approach in an established 3D simulator (e.g., CARLA) with realistic camera/LiDAR pipelines to test transfer.  
2. Compare against imitation-based agents and hand-coded rule baselines on the same metrics (e.g., safety distance, signal obedience).  
3. Introduce richer action spaces to allow lane changes and merging behaviors to emerge.  
4. Analyze sensitivity to reward weighting and communication channel capacity.

• The 2D physics and simplified LiDAR model limit realism; it is unclear if these findings transfer to high-fidelity 3D simulators with realistic sensors and dynamics.  
• No direct comparison to imitation learning or rule-based baselines (e.g., supervised driving policies) to quantify the benefit of emergent rules.  
• The bilevel spline/acceleration formulation prevents lane-change emergence and may overconstrain agent behavior.

Suggestions:
1. Evaluate the approach in an established 3D simulator (e.g., CARLA) with realistic camera/LiDAR pipelines to test transfer.  
2. Compare against imitation-based agents and hand-coded rule baselines on the same metrics (e.g., safety distance, signal obedience).  
3. Introduce richer action spaces to allow lane changes and merging behaviors to emerge.  
4. Analyze sensitivity to reward weighting and communication channel capacity.","rating":"6: Marginally above acceptance threshold"}

reviews.review: This paper presents VISTA-Net, a unified probabilistic framework that integrates structured spatial and channel attention for dense pixel-wise prediction. The key idea is to model the attention tensor as a sum of T rank-one tensor products of spatial maps and channel vectors, and to embed this within a CRF-style graphical model whose variational inference steps are implemented as neural network modules. Strengths: 1) Novel formulation: combining low-rank tensor structure with variational attention gates is an elegant way to capture joint spatial-channel dependencies. 2) Joint learning: embedding inference updates in the network enables end-to-end training of both attention parameters and CNN front-end. 3) Extensive experiments: evaluation on six large-scale datasets (semantic segmentation, depth, normals) demonstrates consistent improvements over established baselines (DANet, AG-CRF, SANet, DORN). 4) Ablations: thorough analysis of attention variants, tensor rank, message-passing steps confirms the benefits of structured attention and probabilistic inference. Weaknesses: 1) Presentation complexity: the dense notation and multi-factor variational updates could impede understanding and reproducibility; a more streamlined pseudocode or diagrammatic summary would help. 2) Computational overhead: while SOTA results are achieved, the paper reports only raw FPS without a breakdown of added inference cost; more analysis of latency and memory impact is needed. 3) Rank selection: guidance on choosing the tensor rank T for new tasks is limited. 4) Limited discussion of failure modes or scenarios where structured attention offers less benefit. Actionable suggestions: a) Include a concise algorithm box and simplified notation to improve clarity. b) Provide detailed runtime and memory comparisons against key baselines. c) Add heuristics or ablation-driven guidelines for selecting T. d) Incorporate qualitative visualizations of attention maps and failure cases to deepen insight.

reviews.review:
This paper introduces VISTA-Net, a structured attention mechanism modeled as the sum of T rank-1 tensors (spatial map ⊗ channel vector) within a CRF-style probabilistic framework, and implements variational inference updates as network layers. The method is evaluated on six large-scale dense prediction datasets (depth, segmentation, normals), consistently outperforming state-of-the-art models including DANet, SANet, and AG-CRF.

Strengths:
• Novelty: Jointly structuring spatial and channel attention via low-rank tensor decomposition, integrated into a unified probabilistic model. This extends CRF gating (Xu et al. 2017a) to richer attention tensors.  
• Strong empirical gains: Consistent improvement (0.5–1.0% mIoU, lower depth error) across diverse tasks and datasets.  
• End-to-end inference: Unrolling variational updates as differentiable layers enables joint CNN and attention learning.  
• Ablations: Study of rank T and separate spatial/channel variants highlights benefits of the full model.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Clarity of inference schedule: The number of variational update iterations and convergence criteria are not clearly stated. Please specify and ablate iteration depth.  
2. Computational cost: While Appendix shows FPS vs. T, overhead remains high. Discuss trade-offs and show performance/speed for lower T in real-time settings.  
3. Missing baselines: Compare to non-local/self-attention models (e.g. Non-Local Networks) to position against generic attention.  
4. Theoretical analysis: Provide insight on approximation error of the mean-field factorization and its impact on attention quality.  
5. Reproducibility: Release code and detailed hyperparameters (e.g. number of EM steps).

Overall, VISTA-Net is a compelling extension of structured attention for pixel-wise tasks. Addressing clarity and efficiency concerns would further strengthen the work.

reviews.review:
This paper proposes VISTA-Net, a unified structured attention mechanism for dense pixel-wise prediction that jointly models spatial and channel dependencies as a low-rank tensor within a CRF-inspired variational framework. Strengths: 1. The integration of structured attention as a sum of rank-1 tensors is clearly motivated and elegantly cast into a variational inference procedure implemented via standard neural network operations. 2. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks across tasks (segmentation, depth, normals) demonstrate consistent gains over strong baselines (DANet, SANet, MS-CRF, AG-CRF) and set new state-of-the-art results. 3. The ablation study shows the value of both the structured tensor formulation and the probabilistic treatment. 4. The paper is generally well written with detailed derivations and clear diagrams.

Weaknesses: 1. Novelty is limited: structured attention and attention-gated CRFs have been explored in prior work; the main contribution is combining them with low-rank tensor factorization. 2. The choice of tensor rank T lacks theoretical justification and only a single ablation on one dataset is presented; it is unclear how to select T in practice. 3. Computational cost is high (under 1 FPS on Pascal-Context) and memory overhead of the CRF updates is not analyzed. 4. Some implementation details are missing: the initialization and convergence behavior of the variational updates, and sensitivity to hyperparameters (e.g., weighting factors b) are not reported.

Actionable suggestions: 1. Provide guidance on choosing T and include rank-sensitivity on additional tasks. 2. Report GPU memory usage and run-time on all benchmarks, and compare to transformer-style self-attention in efficiency. 3. Clarify initialization of latent variables, update scheduling, and convergence criteria. 4. Discuss limitations when scaling to higher resolution or more modalities.

This paper presents VISTA-Net, a novel integration of structured spatial- and channel-wise attention within a variational CRF framework for dense pixel-wise prediction. The key idea is to model the full attention tensor as a sum of rank-1 outer products of spatial maps and channel vectors, and to infer these latent variables jointly with CRF kernels via a variational approximation implemented as differentiable neural operations. The approach is evaluated on six large-scale datasets (NYUD-V2, KITTI, Pascal-Context, VOC2012, Cityscapes, ScanNet) across depth estimation, semantic segmentation, and surface normal prediction, consistently outperforming recent state-of-the-art baselines (DANet, SANet, AG-CRF, etc.).

Strengths:
• Unified probabilistic formulation: elegantly combines structured attention and CRF inference in an end-to-end trainable network.  
• Empirical gains: consistent improvements (0.5–3% mIoU or accuracy gains) across diverse tasks and datasets.  
• Ablation studies: demonstrate the importance of (i) joint spatial+channel structure versus separate, (ii) variational formulation versus heuristic attention.

Weaknesses:
• Computational overhead: training and inference costs rise with tensor rank T; detailed runtime comparisons versus competing methods (beyond FPS on Pascal-Context) are limited.  
• Hyperparameter sensitivity: the choice of rank T is critical; guidance on selecting T or automatically adapting it is missing.  
• Clarity on convergence: theoretical or empirical analysis of variational approximation convergence (iterations required, stability) is lacking.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Report end-to-end latency and memory for all tasks and compare against baselines under identical hardware.  
2. Include a study of rank T selection (e.g., validation curves, diminishing returns) and consider an adaptive scheme.  
3. Provide convergence plots for the variational updates (number of iterations versus performance) and, if necessary, strategies to reduce inference iterations.  
4. Discuss limitations: scenarios with highly correlated channels or spatial patterns where rank-1 decomposition may be insufficient.  

Overall, the paper proposes a sound and well-validated method. With additional analysis of computational trade-offs and approximation behavior, it would make a strong contribution to dense prediction.

reviews.review: This paper tackles the important problem of sample-efficient automated hyperparameter and architecture optimization for off-policy deep RL. The authors propose SEARL, a population-based framework that evolves both network architecture and algorithm hyperparameters online, while sharing a single replay buffer across agents to dramatically improve meta-optimization sample efficiency. Strengths: (1) Novel combination of evolutionary search, dynamic hyperparameter scheduling, and shared experience yields up to 10× fewer environment interactions for meta-optimization compared to baselines. (2) Clear evaluation protocol that accounts for total interactions, and thorough ablations demonstrating the impact of each component. (3) Demonstrated generality by tuning both TD3 on MuJoCo and Rainbow DQN on Atari. (4) Open-source code and detailed pseudocode aid reproducibility. Weaknesses: (1) Baselines are limited to random search and a modified PBT; comparisons to gradient-based HPO or recent metagradient methods would strengthen claims. (2) The synchronous evolutionary loop may limit scalability; runtime and wall-clock costs are not reported. (3) SEARL underperforms on environments with very short episodes (Reacher, Freeway), suggesting sensitivity to evaluation frequency. (4) The paper lacks theoretical insight into why shared replay yields such gains. Suggestions: (a) Include a comparison to Bayesian or metagradient HPO approaches. (b) Report compute cost and time-to-result versus baselines. (c) Explore an asynchronous variant to improve scalability. (d) Analyze how hyperparameter schedules adapt across tasks and initializations. Overall, SEARL provides a compelling and practical advance in AutoRL, with strong empirical gains.
