reviews.review:
Summary:
This manuscript introduces SEARL, a population-based AutoRL framework that jointly optimizes hyperparameters and neural architectures of off-policy deep RL agents while training. Key innovations include dynamic configuration via evolutionary mutations, online architecture adaptation using Lamarckian principles, and most critically, a shared replay buffer across the population to dramatically improve sample efficiency of meta-optimization. The approach is instantiated on TD3 with MuJoCo and on Rainbow DQN with Atari, demonstrating up to 10× fewer environment interactions than random search or Population-Based Training (PBT) for a given performance level.

Strengths:
• Clear motivation: tackles RL hyperparameter sensitivity and non-stationarity with online HPO and NAS.
• Shared experience replay is a simple yet powerful idea that substantially boosts sample reuse across the population.
• Thorough empirical evaluation: five MuJoCo tasks and five Atari games, with baselines (random search, PBT), ablations, robustness to random seeds, held-out splits, and architecture schedules.
• Open-source release.
• Ablation study convincingly attributes gains to each component, especially shared replay.

Weaknesses:
• Baselines omit more recent AutoRL methods (e.g., Bayesian HPO, metagradient tuning, Runge et al. 2019) and off-policy gradient baselines (e.g., HOOF), limiting context.
• SEARL’s synchronous evolutionary loop may incur higher wall-clock costs; runtime comparisons and resource usage are not reported.
• Experiments are limited to simulated benchmarks; real-world or high-dimensional tasks (e.g., robotics) would strengthen applicability claims.
• The hyperparameter search space and mutation rates appear heuristic; sensitivity to these design choices is not fully explored.
• The framework is restricted to off-policy methods; discussion on on-policy extension is cursory.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Include one or two additional baselines (Bayesian optimization or metagradients) to contextualize gains beyond random/PBT.
2. Report wall-clock time or compute cost to confirm that sample-efficiency gains translate to practical speedups.
3. Evaluate on at least one real-world or higher-dimensional continuous control task to validate real-world viability.
4. Analyze sensitivity to mutation operators and population size to guide practitioners on hyperparameter choices.
5. Expand discussion on extending SEARL to on-policy algorithms or mixed populations.

Overall, SEARL offers a compelling, practical approach to dynamic AutoRL with strong empirical gains in sample efficiency. With additional baselines and runtime analysis, this work would make a valuable contribution to ICLR.

reviews.review: This manuscript proposes SEARL, a population-based AutoRL framework that jointly optimizes hyperparameters and neural architectures of off-policy RL agents using shared experience replay. Strengths include: (1) A clear identification of sample-efficiency and non-stationarity challenges in RL HPO. (2) A novel combination of evolutionary mutation, dynamic hyperparameter schedules, and Lamarckian network growth, with a shared replay buffer to reuse environment samples across agents. (3) Comprehensive empirical validation on MuJoCo (TD3) and Atari (Rainbow DQN) benchmarks, demonstrating up to 10× reduction in environment interactions over random search and modified PBT. (4) Ablation studies disentangling contributions of shared replay, architecture adaptation, hyperparameter tuning, and weight mutation. (5) A fair evaluation protocol accounting for total environment steps. Weaknesses and suggestions: (1) Baseline comparisons omit recent metagradient and bandit-based HPO methods; adding HOOF or Zahavy et al. would strengthen claims. (2) Computational and wall-clock cost of SEARL vs. asynchronous methods is not analyzed; report GPU/CPU hours and parallel efficiency. (3) Architecture search is limited to MLP depth and width; consider richer search spaces (e.g., convolutional or attention modules) or NAS baselines. (4) Robustness to population size, mutation rates, and learning-rate schedules is not fully explored; include sensitivity analyses. (5) On-policy algorithms are not evaluated; a small case study on SAC or PPO would demonstrate generality. Addressing these points will enhance the rigor and broader applicability of the framework.

reviews.review:
This manuscript presents SEARL, a population-based AutoRL framework that integrates dynamic hyperparameter tuning and neural architecture search for off-policy deep RL, using a shared replay buffer to improve sample efficiency. The authors demonstrate up to a 10× reduction in environment interactions for meta‐optimization of TD3 on MuJoCo and extend to DQN on Atari.

Strengths:
1. Well-motivated: addresses RL non-stationarity and high sample cost of hyperparameter search.  
2. Simple, general framework: combines tournament‐based evolution, network mutations, and shared replay.  
3. Empirical gains: consistent 5–10× speedup versus random search and modified PBT across five MuJoCo tasks.  
4. Ablation and protocol: fair interaction‐count evaluation and detailed component analyses.

Weaknesses:
1. Novelty: SEARL is an incremental combination of PBT, evolutionary RL, and shared replay; key contributions over ERL/PBT are modest.  
2. Baselines: missing comparison to gradient-based dynamic HPO (e.g., metagradients, HOOF) and Bayesian optimization with early stopping.  
3. Statistical rigor: evaluations use only 5–10 seeds; confidence bounds on final gains are limited.  
4. Practical overhead: wall–clock training cost and scalability to real‐world tasks are not reported.

Actionable Suggestions:
• Include comparisons to metagradient HPO and other recent adaptive tuning methods.  
• Report wall‐clock compute and GPU usage to assess real‐world applicability.  
• Increase seed count and apply statistical tests on final performance.  
• Evaluate on an on‐policy algorithm or real‐robot environment to demonstrate broader utility.  
Overall, SEARL offers a useful empirical study of joint AutoRL, but the novelty and validation could be strengthened.

reviews.review:
This paper proposes Skip-Window (SkipW), a flexible wrapper for recurrent cells that enforces a strict upper bound on the number of time‐step updates per window of inputs. By computing a per‐window importance vector, selecting the top K entries, and applying a straight‐through binarization, SkipW guarantees that at most K of every L inputs are processed. The authors evaluate on four benchmarks (HAR, sequential MNIST, IMDB, Adding Task) and demonstrate that SkipW matches or outperforms SkipRNN and ThrRNN across accuracy/computation trade‐offs, while providing strict per‐window cost guarantees. They further show real speedups and energy savings on edge hardware and the ability to adapt budgets mid‐sequence.

Strengths:
• Novel mechanism with strict budget enforcement per interval, addressing inter‐ and intra‐sequence variability.
• Comprehensive empirical evaluation on diverse tasks, including real‐device latency and energy measurements.
• Solid comparison to baselines and ablations of window size, thresholding, and sampling variants.

Weaknesses:
• The tasks are all relatively small‐scale; applicability to large vocabulary speech or machine translation remains untested.
• The gating overhead and memory cost of the importance vector are not profiled.
• Hyperparameter selection for L and λ is nontrivial; guidance is limited.

Actionable Suggestions:
1 Provide complexity and memory overhead analysis for the gating and topK operations.
2 Evaluate on at least one larger real‐world sequence task (e.g., speech or language modeling).
3 Include a more detailed discussion or heuristic for choosing window size L relative to sequence statistics.
4 Release code and pretrained models to facilitate reproducibility.

Overall, SkipW represents a meaningful advance in resource‐aware RNNs and is suitable for acceptance.

reviews.review:
This paper presents Skip-Window (SkipW), a flexible wrapper for RNN cells that enforces a strict upper bound on per-window updates via a TopK gating mechanism. Strengths:
• Novelty: Unlike prior methods (SkipRNN, ThrRNN) that only constrain average updates, SkipW guarantees a hard budget per window, making it well suited to real-time edge deployment. 
• Empirical validation: Extensive experiments on four datasets (Adding Task, sequential MNIST, IMDB, HAR-2D-POSE) demonstrate that SkipW matches or outperforms baselines at lower computational cost. Ablations confirm the importance of TopK and thresholding, and real-device measurements on Jetson and Raspberry Pi show substantial latency and energy savings.
• Dynamic adaptation: SkipW can switch operating points mid-sequence, a unique feature for changing runtime budgets.

Weaknesses:
• Baselines: The paper compares only to SkipRNN and ThrRNN; it omits recent hierarchical or summary-based sequence models and early-exit RNNs. 
• Hyperparameter sensitivity: The choice of window size L is dataset-specific and no automatic or theoretical guideline is provided. 
• Limited task scope: Datasets are relatively small or synthetic; no challenging real-world long-sequence tasks (e.g., speech, machine translation) are evaluated. 
• Reproducibility: Key implementation details (exact hyperparameters, code releases) are relegated to appendices.

Actionable suggestions:
1. Compare against additional dynamic RNN baselines (hierarchical RNNs, early exit methods). 
2. Provide heuristics or adaptive schemes to select window size L and K. 
3. Release code, hyperparameter settings, and training scripts. 
4. Evaluate on at least one large-scale real-world sequence benchmark (e.g., speech recognition, longer text).

reviews.review: This paper introduces Skip-Window, a wrapper for RNN cells that enforces a strict budget of processed inputs over fixed-size windows by computing update gates only at window boundaries and using a top-K selection mechanism. Strengths: (1) The core idea of bounding computation within each window addresses a real need for predictable runtimes on edge devices and extends prior SkipRNN/ThrRNN methods by eliminating intra- and inter-sequence variability. (2) The method is generic (cell-agnostic) and simple to integrate with LSTM/GRU. (3) Empirical evaluation spans four benchmarks (Adding Task, sequential MNIST, IMDB, HAR) and includes real hardware latency and energy measurements on Jetson and Raspberry Pi, highlighting practical gains. (4) The ability to change the K or threshold at inference time is demonstrated, enabling dynamic trade-off adjustments mid-sequence. Weaknesses: (1) Novelty is incremental over existing skip-update RNNs; the top-K gating is a straightforward extension. (2) The method adds two new hyperparameters (window size L, top-K), whose tuning and interaction are not fully explored, and may require per-task labor. (3) Performance gains on some tasks (e.g. sequential MNIST) are modest or comparable to baselines, and no large-scale or language modeling benchmarks are tested. (4) Gradient estimation through top-K and binary gating relies on straight-through approximations; stability and sensitivity to initializations are not analyzed. Suggestions: (i) Provide ablation on sensitivity to L and K across more tasks and guidance on choosing them. (ii) Compare to hierarchical or dilated RNN gating baselines beyond ThrRNN. (iii) Analyze gradient signal through top-K: report training variance and convergence details. (iv) Include a larger real-world sequence modeling task (e.g. language or speech) to strengthen generality.

reviews.review: This paper introduces Skip-Window (SkipW), a window-based extension to RNNs that enforces a strict upper bound on per-window computation by selecting the top-K most important inputs in each L-sized window. The authors demonstrate that SkipW matches or outperforms existing flexible RNN methods (SkipRNN, ThrRNN) on four benchmarks (HAR, sequential MNIST, IMDB, adding task) while providing real latency and energy savings on edge devices (Jetson Nano/TX2, Raspberry Pi). Strengths: • Novelty: By shifting gating decisions to fixed windows and enforcing a top-K constraint, SkipW eliminates intra- and inter-sequence variability in compute cost—a practical advantage for real-time, resource-constrained settings. • Empirical validation: Extensive experiments illustrate superior accuracy-cost trade-offs, adaptability of trade-off parameters at inference time, and real hardware gains. • Clarity: The paper clearly motivates the need for strict compute budgets on edge devices and thoroughly compares to relevant baselines. Weaknesses: • Incremental extension: SkipW builds on existing SkipRNN/ThrRNN architectures; the conceptual contribution (windowed top-K gating) is modest. • Hyperparameter sensitivity: Performance depends strongly on window size L, top-K, and threshold thr, requiring potentially costly tuning per task. • Limited tasks and analysis: Datasets are relatively small; there is no theoretical analysis of convergence or overhead of gating. Actionable suggestions: 1. Provide an expanded ablation on window size and gating overhead, including training/inference time and memory cost of the top-K operation itself. 2. Compare to hierarchical RNNs and other budget-aware methods on naturalistic, larger-scale sequence tasks (e.g., language modeling). 3. Analyze sensitivity to L, K, and thr, and propose guidelines for setting them. 4. Consider lightweight approximations to top-K to reduce gating overhead. Overall, SkipW offers a practical advancement for enforceable budgets in RNNs, but broader evaluation and deeper analysis would strengthen the contribution.
